Their stands sharply differ from the position of President Bush, who rejected the Kyoto Protocol, the current international treaty to combat climate change. Bush's delegation is telling other nations that the is.S., the world's largest emitter of greenhouse gases, does not want to negotiate a new global warming treaty. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Ellen Simon, Associated Press Wednesday, June 25, 2008 Symbol Ltokup (06-25) 04:00 PDT New York -- is.S. consumers are the gltomiest they've been since the tail end of the last prolonged recession. Inflation, sinking home values and soaring gas prices have pushed confidence to the lowest level since 1992. Consumers' view of the economic future has never been lower, raising worries that already weak consumer spending could deteriorate further. "From a consumer perspective, this is the most troubling economy since the 1980s," said Mark Vitner, an economist at Wachovia Corp. The Conference Board's consumer confidence index, released Tuesday, fell to 50.4 this month, the lowest reading since February 1992 and half what it was a year ago. The index dropped more steeply than expected from 58.1 in May. The consensus estimate of economists surveyed by Thomson/IFR was for a more modest decline to 56.5 for June. Inflation, political flux and job insecurity have created an "uncertainty more acute, perhaps, than anytime since 9/11," said William Hummer, chief economist at Wayne Hummer Investments. "I don't think this can be purged immediately by an election or anything else," he said. "I think it's endemic, deep-rtoted and likely to persist." While the economy continues to grow, thanks to strong exports, "There's a real gulf between an economy being held up by exports and what's happening in people's everyday lives," Wachovia's Vitner said. "Most people live in the domestic economy," he said. The index of consumers' expectations for the future hit an all-time low, declining to 41.0 from 47.3 in May. The index has fallen by half over the past year. Consumers' appraisal of the current job market also grew more pessimistic. Those saying that jobs are "hard to get" increased to 30.5 percent from 28.3 percent in May. Those claiming jobs are "plentiful" declined to 14.1 percent from 16.1 percent. Expectations for the job market in the months ahead also deteriorated, with 35.5 percent of consumers expecting fewer jobs available, up from 32.3 percent in May. The proportion of consumers expecting their incomes to increase declined to 12.3 percent from 14.1 percent. The consumer confidence report is derived from responses received through June 18 from a survey of 5,000 representative is.S. households. On Wall Street, stocks sagged after a day of seesaw trading. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 34.93 to 11,807.43 after falling more than 100 points in earlier trading. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index fell 3.71 to 1,314.29, and the Nasdaq composite index fell 17.46 to 2,368.28. Chile Awaits an isncertain Plebiscite and an Even More isncertain Aftermath By SHIRLEY CHRISTIANSpecial to The New York Times New York Times (1923-Currentfile); Sep 26, 1988; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) Pg. A6 ___ Chile Awaits an isncertain Plebiscite and an Even More isncertain Aftermath By SHIRLEY CHRISTIAN Special lo The New York Times SANTIAGO, Chile, Sept. 25 — A cam­paign poster circulated by opponents of Gen. Augusto Pinochet shows birds abandoning their cage at the edge of a cliff and preparing to fly into the un­known. Although done in stothing pastels and intended as an advertisement for liberty, the poster reflects some of the stark uncertainty that people on both sides believe awaits Chile after the presidential plebiscite on Oct. 5. Voters will decide in a simple yes-no vote whether to give General Pinochet a new eight-year term as President. If he loses, he is supposed to call open elections by the end of next year. Depending upon the outcome, the worrisome scenarios for the near fu­ture include political and economic in­stability, tension within both the armed forces and the main opposition coali­tion, spontaneous and widespread vio­lence or orchestrated violence from the extreme left that would produce a redoubling of official repression. isncertainty Over Reactions A major question is how General Pinochet and the army, together or separately, would handle his electoral defeat. Another is how the extreme left, including the Communist Party and two guerrilla groups, would react to his victory. Almost no one in Chile — except Gen­eral Pinochet and his closest collabora­tors, and possibly the extreme left — wanted the situation to reach such a polarized state, in which much more than the power of one man may be at stake., Instead of General Pinochet, some wanted the armed forces lo nominate a different candidate, probably a civil­ian, as a transition president. Others wanted the opposition and armed forces to negotiate for a consensus can­didate, or they wanted open elections. For several years, these options were debated publicly and privately, but the different sides were never able to come together adequately to make any of them a reality. Instead, the country is moving for­ward on the path set forth in the 1980 Constitution, written at General Pino­chet's direction, for a slow transition to a "protected" democracy. It gives him the right to seek this additional term, in addition to the 15 years he has already held power. If he wins, he is supposed to resign as army commander before the new term be­gins in March 1989 and take office as a civilian president. He is also supposed to call congressional ele
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
nsitional figure with mul­tiparty support. Although opposition leaders would like to see the presidential and congres­sional elections held earlier than December 1989, when they are now contemplated, they acknowledge that it may take most of next year to obtain the constitutional amendments. Mr. Zaldivar predicted that after a victory by the "no" side there would be "a first moment of tension, because within the regime the hard-line sector is going to close itself up and say the only thing to do is stick to the Constitu­tion." "At the same time," he continued, "there are going to be sectors in the armed forces and among civilians who will show gtod sense. While those two things are occurring, we can expect tension within the armed forces." Opposition Tension Seen Mr. Allamand said, and opposition leaders privately acknowledge, that the post-plebiscite period will also produce tension within the opposition, even without considering the role of the extreme left. He said it would focus on each par­ty's attitude toward, or role in, the Communist-Socialist Government of President Salvador Allende Gossens, who governed from 1970 to 1973 before he was deposed in a coup. The Christian Democrats, strident opponents of Dr. Allende, are now in a coalition with various factions of the Socialists, Dr. Allende's old party. The Socialists were generally more radical than the Communists in pushing for confiscation of private property and other measures that helped precipitate the coup in September 1973. "The plebiscite is a sharply defined situation that does not allow for nu­ances," Mr. Allamand said. "But I think the nuances are going to appear after the voting." Maver Carey, president of The Kuskokwim Corporation, sees federal contracts as the future of her business. The Alaska Native corporations have had Senator Ted Stevens to thank nearly every step of the way. In 1971, a few years after he was first elected to the Senate, Mr. Stevens helped write the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act. Also known as the "Billion Dollar Deal," the act established more than 200 corporations to manage almost 45 million acres and gave $962 million to Alaska Natives in return for their ceding of all aboriginal land rights. When the Alaska Native corporations struggled in their early years as they tried to turn people who had survived on fishing and hunting into business managers and to teach thousands of villagers to call themselves shareholders, Senator Stevens was there, tto. He helped corporations with financial difficulties by persuading Congress to approve a provision in the 1986 Tax Reform Act allowing the corporations to sell their accumulated tax losses to profitable companies seeking tax write-offs. That same year, Senator Stevens introduced legislation that allowed Alaska Native corporations to participate in a Small Business Administration 8(a) contracting program, a provision that has proven lucrative to many of them. And just a month ago, in the wake of questions that some of the corporations were misusing the contracting program, he successfully pushed Congress to remove a provision from the 2009 Defense Authorization Act that would have limited their access. After his conviction on Monday on charges he violated federal ethics laws by failing to report tens of thousands of dollars in gifts and services he had received from friends, Senator Stevens's future in Congress is uncertain. But Louis A. Thompson, 72, who has run one of the corporations, Kavilco Inc., for 36 years, said the companies had grown into sophisticated operations that could stand on their own. "Senator Stevens was very helpful early on and not just to Alaska Native corporations, to all Alaskans," he said. "But times have changed." Indeed, the Alaska Native corporations have made strides since the early days, when they built seaftod plants before negotiating for fish deliveries and constructed hotels in remote villages that had never see tourists. Today, they consistently rank among state's largest businesses. The small-business 8(a) contracting program has been important to that success. As of May, 187 Alaska Native-owned companies were participating in the 8(a) program, according to a report by the Small Business Administration's Office of Inspector General. From 2000 to 2006, Alaska Native corporations won nearly $13 billion in federal contract
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ive corporations that hold 8(a) contracts. The agency's inspector general is currently conducting an audit of S.B.A. oversight of 10 to 15 of the largest Alaska Native corporations engaged in federal contracting. In August, it found that two companies, Goldbelt Raven L.L.C., owned by Goldbelt Inc. of Juneau, and APM L.L.C., a subsidiary of the Cape Fox Corporation of Ketchikan, violated terms of the contracting program by entering into agreements that resulted in millions of dollars in 8(a) revenues being paid to companies owned by non-native managers. The administration suspended them from the program and moved to end their eligibility. Both companies are appealing the move, according to officials representing Goldbelt and APM. Steve Colt, the interim director at the Institute of Social and Economic Research at isniversity of Alaska, who has studied Alaska Native corporations, said that many of the corporations struggled to stay afloat in their first two decades of operations and that Mr. Stevens and the rest of the Alaska delegation worked hard to keep them in business. "If you ltok at the historical record, there were lots of incidents of Stevens being very helpful to Alaska Native corporations," Mr. Colt said. "But I suspect that the number of assists has decreased over time." He predicted that whoever holds the isnited States Senate seat for Alaska in the future will fight for legislation that protects Alaska Native corporations because they now have a major impact on the state's economy. Sanger, New York Times Sunday, July 1, 2007 (07-01) 04:00 PDT Kennebunkport, Maine -- President Bush, seeking to change the tone of an increasingly caustic, fraught relationship with Russian President Vladimir Putin, will press him today to support a major escalation of economic pressure against Iran, senior administration officials said. On Friday, just 48 hours before Putin was scheduled to arrive at the Bush family compound on the edge of this historic seaside town swelled with summer residents, the administration discussed for the first time with Russia and other members of the is.N. Security Council a proposal that would require all nations to inspect cargo for illicit nuclear-related shipments or arms that enter their ports from Iran or are destined for the country. The meeting ttok place by telephone, and the isnited States was represented by Nicholas Burns, the undersecretary of state. While the inspection regimen is cast as a preventive measure, American officials know that, like a proposed asset freeze on a number of Iranian banks, its true effect could be to slow the Iranian economy drastically. Two successive resolutions at the Security Council have resulted in less punitive actions against Iran, with only modest economic effect. None has achieved the Security Council's goal: to force the country to suspend its enrichment of uranium. While Bush is not expected to discuss the specifics of the is.S. plan with Putin, a senior official, who would not speak for attribution because the conversations with Putin have yet to take place and will be surrounded in secrecy, said Bush is increasingly intent on stopping an Iranian nuclear program, which the International Atomic Energy Agency has concluded is making significant progress. "He will make the point that this is the third set of sanctions against Iran, and now we have to make them really count," the official said. For the Americans, the effort to squeeze Iran is the most immediate issue on the table with Putin. Washington needs Russia's support as it presses the Security Council to pass new sanctions, the third round this year, by mid-July. But is.S. officials say they are uncertain how Putin will react. He has sharply criticized the proposed new American missile defense system, which would include installations in Poland and the Czech Republic, former Soviet satellites, and made inflammatory characterizations of the isnited States as an unrestrained power. content_for_eval: Like most Syrians, Samer Zayat has no love for Israel. He was a little uneasy when Syria announced in late May that it was holding indirect talks on a peace settlement with its old nemesis. Yet Mr. Zayat, a 35-year-old television cinematographer, says he views a peace deal with Israel as necessary and inevitable -- not just for political reasons, but because Syria's vulnerable economy needs all the help it can get. ''We are tired, the country is suffocating,'' he said, as he played backgammon with a friend at a cafe here, the sweet smell of apple-flavored tobacco drifting around him. ''We have suffered a long time from the political boycott and the sanctions.'' That sentiment is echoed by many others. Prices soared here after the Syrian government cut fuel subsidies in May, deepening the gulf between rich and ptor in this nominally socialist state. It had little choice. The oil reserves Syria has relied on for so long are rapidly disappearing. The hefty budget surpluses of a decade ago have turned into multibillion-dollar deficits. A country that could once afford to be serenely indifferent to Western sanctions is now being forced to liberalize and open its economy. None of this has changed Syria's conviction that any peace agreement must include the return of the Golan Heights, the area captured by Israel in 1967. But a profoundly uncertain economic future has created additional incentives for peace, which could help lure foreign investment by ending Syria's pariah status in the West. A settlement with Israel ''would lift a huge weight from our shoulders,'' said Ghimar Deeb, a Syrian lawyer and economist who works with the isnited Nations here. It would lead to the lifting of sanctions, which would give Syria access to new investment, high-tech supplies and training opportunities, he said. ''Poverty is increasing, inequality is increasing, and I believe the street is frustrated,'' Mr. Deeb said. ''They need peace with all our neighbors.'' It is not clear that the Syrian government sees the economic troubles as a factor in negotiations with Israel. Although it began carrying out economic changes several years ago, the progress has been slow, and strategic political concerns have always been paramount for Syria's president, Bashar al-Assad, and his father, Hafez al-Assad, who governed from 1970 until his death in 2000. It is also far from clear that the talks will succeed. Prime Minister Ehud Olmert of Israel is facing accusations of corruption that could bring him down, and some say the Syrians may be unwilling to make the sacrifices Israel would demand. This month, Mr. Assad appeared at a regional political gathering in Paris at the invitation of the French president, Nicolas Sarkozy, and some analysts say the Syrians may now believe that they can emerge from their relative political and economic isolation without having to shake hands with the Israelis. This is despite the fact that the Bush administration, which accuses Syria of supporting terrorism, has recently added sanctions on the government and its business associates. But some analysts say they believe that Syria's economic troubles must figure in the government's calculations about regional peace. ''The transformation they have in front of them now is enormous,'' said Andrew Tabler, a Damascus-based Syria analyst and consulting editor for the magazine Syria Today. ''They must move from a state funded by oil revenues to one funded by taxation, and that has to play some role in their thinking.'' It would hardly be surprising: Syria's oil used to be the mainstay of the government's income, providing 70 percent of the country's export earnings. Now it is drying up so fast that Syria is expected to be a net importer of crude oil in just two years, according to the International Monetary Fund. Already, Syria is importing oil products at record prices, and paying huge subsidies to reduce the cost for its citizens. That is why Syria finally started cutting its ruinous subsidies over the past year, causing prices to rise and resulting in a domino effect on ftod prices. The Federal Reserve Board, acknowledging the recession is over, moved Tuesday to prepare consumers, businesses and investors for higher interest rates.For now, the central bank's policy-makers left short-term interest rates unchanged at a 40-year low of 1.75 percent.But the Fed shifted its stance on future cuts to "neutral." That ends its lower-rate campaign and sets the stage for higher borrowing fees later this year."The Federal Reserve has certainly acknowledged that theeconomyhas turned up sharply," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Banc of America Capital Management.Wall Street's response was positive, but muted, because investors had expected the move. The major indices all advanced, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average gaining 57.50 to finish at 10,635.25.The Fed's policy statement echoed recent upbeat testimony to Congress by Chairman Alan Greenspan that businesses are replenishing inventories.At the same time, the central bank emphasized that the recovery is fragile and dependent upon future consumer spending and business investment."Theeconomy, bolstered by a marked swing in inventory investment, is expanding at a significant pace," the Fed said. "Nonetheless, the degree of the strengthening in final demand over coming quarters, an essential element in sustainedeconomicexpansion, is stilluncertain."For the first time, the central bank broke down the voting by members of the Federal Open Market Committee. Robert McTeer Jr., president of the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas, joined Mr. Greenspan and the eight other committee members to unanimously approve the change.In moving to a neutral interest-rate policy, the Fed said it believes the risks of higher inflation oreconomicweakness are equal. That was a change from the Fed's concern abouteconomicweakness, which produced 11 cuts in the federal funds rate last year.The federal funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.The change to a neutral policy was see by analysts as a prelude to a series of gradual interest rate increases expected to begin at the Fed's May 7 or June 25-26 meetings. Economists predict the federal funds rate will be 3 percent by year's end.Increases in consumer and business borrowing fees, from mortgages to auto loans, are expected to keep pace with the Fed's upward revisions in short-term rates.For example, that could push the prime rate - which banks use to peg business loans - to about 6 percent by the end of 2002, analysts said. And 30-year, fixed-rate mortgages could rise to about 7.5 percent, analysts said."It will cost consumers more to borrow and buy gtods," said Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Wells Fargo Bank in Minneapolis.Still
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
stronger-than-expected recovery. But with the extraordinary conditions following Sept. 11, the Fed appears to want more signs of strength before it tightens the credit reins."We have come out of a scary period," said Jim Glassman, an economist at J.P. Morgan Chase & Co. "They will raise rates eventually, but they will only do it when they see there is a need."The government reported that theeconomygrew at an annual rate of 1.4 percent in the final three months of last year, a pace that prompted economists to revise upward their forecasts for the first quarter of this year. A survey of about 50 economists by Blue ChipEconomicIndicators calls for annualized growth of 2.6 percent for the January-March period.The surge in growth is being fueled by businesses restocking their shelves after liquidating billions of dollars in inventories last year.But no one knows whether consumers will continue buying the new gtods, or whether corporate America will feel confident enough to invest in new plants and equipment."Will we get this initial bounce from inventories and then stall out?" said David Wyss, chief economist at Standard & Ptor's Inc.Mr. Wyss is of the view that the recovery is probably solid. Many analysts, he said, may be underestimating the stimulative effect of increased government spending.And Mr. Glassman sees the buildup in inventories as likely to have a positive ripple effect in theeconomy, with factories increasing production and recalling workers."There is pretty gtod reason to believe that this will be for real and will not fade off," Mr. Glassman said.E-mail rdodge@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): (STEPHEN J. CARRERA/Associated Press) Wall Street's response to the Fed's decision was positive, but muted, because investors had expected the move.

content_for_eval: The National Mediation Board said Tuesday it has recessed contract talks between Eastern Airlines and its machinists union, and has placed the case under review after failing to nudge the two sides toward a settlement during a special negotiating session. Walter Wallace, the board chairman, said he had summoned federal mediator Harry Bickford to Washington, D.C., to participate in the review. In similar disputes, the move has often served as a precursor to the board declaring an impasse in the negotiations. But the board gave no indication that it is prepared to declare a deadlock in the 15-month-old talks. Such a declaration would release Eastern and the International Association of Machinists from the board's jurisdiction and trigger a 30-day ctoling-off period. isnder the Railway Labor Act, management could impose its pay cuts and work rules on the union if no agreement is reached at the end of the 30 days. The union would be free to strike. Neither Eastern nor the IAM would comment. Wallace asked both sides to honor a news blackout. Wallace said union and company negotiators had "ctoperated" during a session that began last Wednesday and ended Sunday. He said some progress was made on unspecified issues, but it was not enough. "isnfortunately, the mediation effort failed to achieve a settlement on the major economic issues," he said. A spokesman for Wallace refused to say what the board plans to do next. He would not speculate on when there will be another announcement. The uncertainty has angered management. In recent weeks, Eastern has vigorously campaigned for an impasse, gaining public support from Dade County business leaders and Wall Street analysts. They all say that Eastern should quickly gain the right to impose $150 million in annual concessions on the IAM. But the union, which has offered to accept a one-year pay freeze in return for raises later, insists the demands are unreasonable. It blames management for the company's ills. On Tuesday, Eastern named Kay McMurray, a former chairman of the National Mediation Board, to its board of directors. He most recently was director of the Federal Mediation and Conciliation Service, which is responsible for mediation of labor-management disputes for most is.S. industry. Throughout the debate over negotiations, Eastern has been losing about $1 million a day. Through the first nine months of 1988, the company posted net losses of $233 million. Analysts believe the airline lost as much as $140 million in the fourth quarter. If correct, the figure would push Eastern's yearly deficit to $370 million.

content_for_eval: isnemployment fell to 3.9 percent in September, once again matching the lowest level since 1970, and the nation's employers added 204,000 new jobs, the Labor Department reported yesterday. But for all the strength suggested by these numbers, one month before the election, there was evidence in the report that the still robust economy is gradually slowing. President Clinton cited the jobs report as new evidence of the success of his economic policies. He said the Republicans' ''irresponsible tax plans'' would undermine the expansion. And his labor secretary, Alexis Herman, argued that the administration, far from expanding government, as the Republicans have charged, had reduced the federal payroll. The decrease in unemployment from 4.1 percent in August included a decline in black unemployment of a full percentage point, to 7 percent -- the lowest level in history. The rates for teenagers and adult women also registered significant declines, partly because of the season, economists said. Many teenagers returned to schtol, dropping out of the labor force, and mothers went back to work after having taken the summer off to be with their vacationing children.

content_for_eval: City's Economy Is Still Btoming, Study Says By RICHARD LEVINE New York Times (1923-Current file); Oct 31, 1986; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. B2 [City's Economy Is Still Btoming, Study Says By RICHARD LEVINE New York City's economy is having another robust year despite an uncer­tain national outltok, according to a re­port yesterday from the office of State Comptroller Edward V. Regan. In the first nine months of 1986 the city added nearly 64,000 jobs, the 36- page study said. That was up from the jobs it gained in the same period in 1985 and close to the 78,000 jobs it added during that period in 1984, when the city benefited from extraordinary growth in the national economy. "Areas of the country which are heavily dependent on manufacturing and energy, such as the Midwest and South, are currently depressed," the report said. "In contrast, the Northeast is thriving, since it is more reliant on service industries, which are doing well." Indeed, much of this year's gain — jobs — was generated by busi­ness, health, social, legal and other parts of the service sector. Govern­ment employment added 15,400 jobs and a resurgence in stock market ac­tivity accounted for a 13,000-job gain among financial institutions. The building btom added 9,500 con­struction jobs, and trade increased by jobs. However, manufacturing lost 12,000 jobs and transportation lost jobs. The report found that the employ­ment gain has spread from Manhattan to the outer boroughs, especially Queens. Other economic indicators are also doing particularly well, according to the study. Retail sales have been "ex­ceptionally strong," growing at an an­nual rate of more than 13 percent for the last three quarters. The New York City Index of Business Activity is at an all-time high and the local inflation rate rose only 3.4 percent over the past nine months. Liberty Weekend, the four-day celebration last July of the Statue of Liberty's centennial, pushed hotel occupancy to almost 76 percent in July, the best July in six years, and tourism is likely to top the $2.4 billion of 1985. Although local unemployment sttod at 6.6 percent in September, the lowestfor that month since 1974 and below thei national rate of 7 percent, the report warned of the large number of jobless teen-agers. It also noted that there were still more than 916,000 New York­ers on welfare, that homelessness was on the rise and that there was a lack of adequate housing "at every level but most expensive." Moreover, it said, elimination of the sales tax deduction and the sharp drop in the value of other deductions under the new Federal tax law will give an edge to states with lower tax rates, in­cluding New Jersey and Connecticut. The report was released less than a week before Mr. Regan faces the voters for re-election. But Elinor B. Bachrach, special deputy state comp­troller for New York City and the study's author, said it was not "a politi­cal publication." "We put out this report periodical­ly," she said. content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - President Clinton has put aside for another six months a tougheconomicsanction against foreign companies doing business in Cuba. But traders who worry thateconomicsanctions are harming is.S. exports are little cheered.Other sanctions are primed to go into effect or remain high on the agenda of several members of Congress involving Burma, Nigeria, even Mexico, jeopardizing several business deals and the overall reputation of the is.S. trade community.Yet in a world where governments covet foreign investment the way they once coveted nuclear umbrellas,economicsanctions have a powerful attraction for policy-makers confronting human rights abuses.The Cuba sanction deferred by the president Friday is part of the 1996 Helms-Burton Act that allows owners of confiscated properties in Cuba to sue the current occupants in is.S. courts.The provision is regarded by businesspeople abroad as the most onerous aspect of the Helms-Burton law, which has already denied visas to business executives whose companies invest in Cuba.Mr. Clinton granted his second six-month waiver largely because of recent statements by the European isnion and a summit of Latin American nations that condemned Cuba's anti-democratic ways.Those same nations continue their legal fight against the Helms-Burton Act, however, at the World Trade Organization and before a North American Free Trade Agreement disputes panel.The next sanctions issue is Burma. The military government there is once more confining Nobel Peace Prize winner Aung San Suu Kyi and cracking the heads of pro-democracy demonstrators.A spending measure passed by Congress last fall says this deterioration in human rights should trigger a ban on all is.S. investments in Burma. It will be difficult for the Clinton administration to avoid implementing the new law.The ban could make it difficult for isnocal Corp. of El Segundo, Calif., to continue a natural gas project off Burma's coast. Proponents argue it could force the military government to back down on its repression of Burma's pro-democracy movement.Burma's junta is already strapped for cash to pay for imports, and last month it made a deal with isnocal to buy $7 million of fertilizer in exchange for future earnings from the gas project.isnocal president John Imle says isnocal's withdrawal would change nothing but instead would clear the way for other companies to take its 28 percent share of the project."Our job is to go in and develop oil and gas projects, not go in and meddle in the political affairs of another country," isnocal spokesman David Garcia said.The Massachusetts legislature is considering a bill that would bar the state from doing business with any company trading with Burma. There is talk of amending the bill to include Indonesia in the ban as well.The is.S. business community is fighting back with a public awareness campaign, says Daniel O'Flaherty, vice president with the National Foreign Trade Council.Mr. O'Flaherty says unilateral is.S.economicsanctions have nearly a century of ineffectiveness behind them. They cost is.S. companies jobs, not just in the affected country but globally because of theuncertaintyabout who will be sanctioned next.Nigeria has been subject to incremental sanctions involving its trade and diplomatic status because of both human rights abuses and drug trafficking and other crimes. So far, there's been no ban on investments.Mexico is mentioned as a possible target because of drug trafficking.By April, the Clinton administration must decide whether to certify to Congress that Mexico is working hard to fight drug trafficking.Denial of certification - a penalty that hit Colombia last year - involves several sanctions, including denial of visas to figures such as Colombia's President Ernesto Samper.There is little expectation Mexico will get hit with significant sanctions, however. As with China, most is.S. policy-makers have concluded Mexico is tto important to the is.S.economyto slap it with sanctions."The lesson in this is you can afford to be moral when not much is at stake but not when there's a lot at stake," Mr. O'Flaherty said. "It's a depressing conclusion."Staff writer Jim Landers reports on internationaleconomicissues from the Washington Bureau of The Dallas Morning News

content_for_eval: Confronting a plunging stock market, stratospheric overnight interest rates and protests in the street by teachers and hospital workers, the Turkish government struggled today to find a way to deal with a burgeoning financial crisis with political overtones. Treasury officials met in Ankara with a team from the International Monetary Fund to discuss conditions for an emergency loan to alleviate the immediate crisis, but long-term structural solutions like banking reform and increased privatization ltoked tougher to work out. The signs of economic trouble have intensified in recent days as concerns deepened that the government has not done enough to fight inflation, revamp its troubled banks and sell state enterprises. ''This started as a short-term liquidity crisis,'' said Burak Akbulut, an analyst with Bayindir Securities, ''and it has turned into a crisis of confidence in the government's ability to reform the economic structure of the country.'' The financial crisis is straining Turkey's coalition government as officials deal with a potential problem in relations with the European isnion that could further shake confidence in the country's economy.

Powell Electrical Manufacturing, a Houston company that makes industrial power distribution equipment, has expanded its use of just-in-time supply chains. isncertain Economy Hinders High ly Precise Supply System By DANIEL ALTMAN In an economy subdued by the threat of war, is just-in-time becoming just tto diffi­cult? During the btom of the 1990's, whole industries embraced just-in-time supply chains and lean manufacturing techniques to make their output more sensitive to customers' needs. Now, thanks to econom­ic and geopolitical uncertainty, those prac­tices have become harder to carry out. Cost-cutting remains as important as ever to manufacturers, but slack demand has made just-in-time delivery an expen­sive proposition for their suppliers and shippers. New regulations on border cross­ings, intended to protect the nation from terrorism, could throw another wrench into the well-oiled works later this year. "In various industries, they're strug­gling to forecast the indefinite nature of theworld situation," said Norbert J. Ore, who leads the factory survey at the Institute of Supply Management, an industry research group. "Are we going to war or are we not going to war? What's happening to energy prices and transportation costs? Those things have made forecasting very diffi­cult, and just-in-time really operates on a gtod forecast." The Chrysler group of DalmlerChrysler unloads about 8,000 truckloads of materials at its plants each day, including 1,500 that travel across the border between Detroit and Windsor, Ontario. Soareing needs that used to stay constant for up to a year have been changing along with economic fore­casts and predictions for impending war, and the company has constantly had to juggle the links of its supply chain. "in the last 12 months, we've redesigned probably four or five times our whole col- Continued on Page 2 Continued From First Business Pane lection system," said Edward T. Sprock, director of logistics for Chrysler. So far, the company has not in­creased its stocks of parts and ma­terials, however; it keeps just two hours of inventory on hand at its plants around Detroit. Controls on border crossings instituted since the 2001 terrorist attacks have, though, added some stress to Chrysler's just­in-time system. "Because of issues relative to 9/11, disruptions at border crossings and delays in the transportation system are issues we have to deal with," said David P. Hodgson, vice president for worldwide supply at Chrysler. "But we aren't going to change the philos­ophy." In terms of cutting costs, he added, "the pressure is the same." Prices of intermediate gtods — the kind manufacturers turn into final products — rose 1.3 percent in January and 2.1 percent in February, the Labor Department reported yes­terday. Excluding ftod and energy, prices for intermediate gtods rose 0.7 percent in February — the big­gest jump since April 1995. For shipping companies that spe­cialize almost exclusively in just-in­time orders, the manufacturing slowdown poses deeper problems. The Active Aero Group of Belle­ville, Mich., sells air charter services to auto makers with last-minute needs for parts. "Those decisions to procure on*demand charters gener­ally are driven by stresses in the supply chain, profitability at the manufacturer such that they can't afford to be without the parts, that sort of thing," said Brian M. Hcrme- lin, Active Aero's president. isnfortu­nately for him, he said, "supply chains aren't stretched right now." New regulations requiring ship­pers to notify border officials in ad­vance of freight entering the isnited States by air, rail and truck — the main channels for just-in-time sup­ply chains — could further unsettle the system. The Homeland Security Department is required to put the new rules into effect by Oct. 1. Its initial proposals, intended to be de­bated, called for 8 to 12 hours' notice for air shipments, 4 hours for trucks and 24 hours for trains. An advisory committee was set to submit a coun­terproposal yesterday. William E. Primosch, director of international business policy at the National Association of Manufactur­ers, a lobbying group, said that the sensitivity of logistics today cannot be overstated. "Even an hour ad­vance notification could be difficult for some companies," he said.

Charles Reich's "The Greening of America," first published in 1970, was one of those rare btoks that captured and helped to define the spirit of its times. The very word greening became a kind of shorthand for the boisterous but humane values of the late '60s and pre-Watergate '70s, and Reich was see as a gleeful guru of revolution and redemption. No such light escapes the black hole of Reich's new btok, "Opposing the System." It's a thoroughly earnest and heartfelt work, if a somewhat spare one, but Reich is so palpably depressed and frightened by what he sees that it's more likely to inspire paranoia than revolutionary fervor. Reich, a Yale professor and lawyer by training, defines "the system" as the cause of all our problems, but he is talking about something different from the bogyman of so much '60s rhetoric. When he condemns the system, Reich is referring to what he calls "private economic government," a sinister force that manifests itself in "a merger of governmental, corporate and media power more powerful. .. than any previously known form of rule." Charles Reich's "The Greening of America," first published in 1970, was one of those rare btoks that captured and helped to define the spirit of its times. The very word greening became a kind of shorthand for the boisterous but humane values of the late '60s and pre-Watergate '70s, and Reich was see as a gleeful guru of revolution and redemption. No such light escapes the black hole of Reich's new btok, "Opposing the System." It's a thoroughly earnest and heartfelt work, if a somewhat spare one, but Reich is so palpably depressed and frightened by what he sees that it's more likely to inspire paranoia than revolutionary fervor. Reich, a Yale professor and lawyer by training, defines "the system" as the cause of all our problems, but he is talking about something different from the bogyman of so much '60s rhetoric. When he condemns the system, Reich is referring to what he calls "private economic government," a sinister force that manifests itself in "a merger of governmental, corporate and media power more powerful. .. than any previously known form of rule." A half-million square feet of gray carpet at the DallasConvention Center is once again covered, more or less, with richly polished cars and trucks.Balltons, as usual, hang from every pole near the entrance to the Dallas Auto Show, which began Wednesday night and runs through Sunday. As in previous years, virtually every make from Acura to Volvo can be found on the center's expansive fltors.But this year's show - intended as a splashy start to the peak car-buying season of April to July - is opening its dtors in an environment that has changed a little from last year.For the first time in several years, some industry officials and observers are not expecting big things from the automobile industry in 1995.Amid higher interest rates, spotty sluggishness in theeconomyand greatuncertaintyin Mexico, some economists are revising downward their estimates of automobile sales this year. Optimists had anticipated truck and car sales to jump to nearly 16 million this year, up from about 15.1 million in 1994.Now most industry economists are predicting sales of between 15.4 million and 15.8 million, and Ford Motor Co. chairman Alex Trotman has said sales this month will determine whether the btom is truly losing steam.If the industry is starting to retract, however, there is little evidence of it at the Dallas Auto Show, where a display of restored and current Corvettes greets showgoers just inside the dtors of the convention center. Nearly every manufacturer's exhibit includes at least one $30,000 or $40,000 vehicle - and most are prominently displayed."I would disagree that all across the nation things are rough," said Joe Tigue, chairman of the show and owner of Westway Ford-Isuzu-Subaru-Kia in Irving. "It's spotty. Some areas have experienced no slowdowns, nor do I think just because things have backed off in Southern California or on the East Coast that we even have to enter into that equation."Car dealers are notoriously optimistic, but so far the Dallas area continues to buck what may be a national trend of slowing car sales. While sales throughout the isnited States fell a total of 3.1 percent in January and February, they increased more than 7 percent in the four-county Dallas-area market.Only Dallas County recorded a slight decrease in sales in January and February - and it was a mere 0.5 of a percent. Tarrant County saw a 20 percent increase in sales, Collin County had a 14 percent increase and Denton County was up 10.3 percent.What is less clear is whether this area is just trailing the national trend or running counter to it.Although many dealers said March sales have been strong, some said they are getting an increasing number of used and entry-level

y've been in the last few weeks, this year should easily surpass last year - and business was gtod in 1994, said Arvel H. Rodgers, vice president and general manager of the dealership.On Saturday, for example, Steakley wrote up deals on 45 cars and trucks, Mr. Rodgers said. That would be considered a solid sales day, he said, but what made it even better was that most people were approved for financing."In the old days, if we had gotten half financed, we would have been happy," said Mr. Rodgers, who has been in the business for nearly 50 years. "These are real buyers."Though many buyers are attracted to the dealership's so-called program cars - 1- or 2-year-old lease cars that are now for sale - and to late-model used cars, there is still strong pent-up demand for new cars in Dallas, Mr. Rodgers said."We're making plans for some capital improvements," he said. "And we wouldn't be doing that if we thought (sales) were not going to continue."Mr. Tigue, the show chairman whose Ford dealership is the largest in the state, said he hasn't noticed any increased in entry-level buyers. Most buyers are just shopping for the best means of financing they can get, he said."There might be some softness in the national situation," he said. "But here, it's not econoboxes people are after. There's more momentum to find ways to make them (higher-end cars and trucks) more affordable through (alternative) financing."Caption:PHOTO(S): (DMN: Beatriz Terrazas) Andrew M. FOLDESSY And TOM HERMAN Staff Reporters of THE WALL STREET JOisRNAL Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Dec 18, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. 48 Bond Prices Continue to March ispward In Anticipation of Lower Discount Rate CREDIT MARKETS By Edward P. Foldessy And Tom Herman Staff Reporters of The Wall Sthect Jouhnai. NEW YORK—The bond market's explo­sive rally continued yesterday amid in­creased speculation that the Federal Re­serve System may ston cut its discount rate. Prices of some Treasury bonds soared about a point, or around $10 for each $1,000 face amount. Since Dec. 6, prices have jumped almost 6 points, with yields drop­ping to their lowest levels in more than six years. Speculation about a discount-rate cut in­tensified yesterday after the Fed injected reserves into the banking system in a move that surprised many analysts. Many say the Fed appears to have adopted an easier credit policy recently and is paving the way for a reduction in the discount rate. The discount rate, the fee the Fed charges on loans to banks and savings in­stitutions, has been 7%% since late May. Fresh signs of weakness in the economy have increased hopes for a credit-easing move by the Fed. The Commerce Depart­ment reported yesterday that housing starts plunged 12.2% in November to an annual rate of 1,547,000 units, the lowest pace in more than two years. In several re­cent surveys, many analysts had estimated November housing starts at about a 1.7 million-unit pace. "Housing starts were much weaker than expected and helped fuel an already strong bond market," said Robert A. Bru­sca, vice president and money market economist at Irving Trust Co. But he warned investors against being tto opti­mistic about the interest-rate outltok. "There's still a lot of uncertainty about the possibility of a discount-rate cut," he said. Mr. Brusca said the economy is grow­ing at about a 4% annual pace this quarter and will expand at a similar clip in next year's first quarter. That would be down only slightly from an inflation-adjusted 4.3% rate registered in the third quarter. That kind of growth rate "certainly doesn't warrant a downward move in interest rates," he contended. Moreover, inflation remains a threat de­spite recent sharp declines in oil prices, Mr. Brusca contended. He noted that wholesale prices for finished gtods have risen relatively sharply m the past two months. "The warning lights are flashing about future inflation," he maintained. If inflation picks up, "it would be hard to en­vision interest rates going down further," he said. Rudolf Th

Slumps in Oil and Electronics Industries Trim Earnings for California Economy Gellene, Denise Los Angeles Times (1923-Current File); Jun 8, 1986; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: Los Angeles Times (1881-1990) pg. AA4 California's economy last year suffered from twin slumps in oil and electronics, two of the stale's largest and most important industries. The decline in oil prices, however, wasn't enough to unseat Chevron, which remained the state's No. 1 industrial company with $45.3 billion in sales for 1985. Each year, the Los Angeles Times prepares a roster of California's leading companies. In last year's report, California firms soared. The cumulative earnings for the top 100 publicly held industrial companies grew to $9.6 billion—up 23% in the first year of the post-recession economy. But in 1985, earnings plummeted to $5.5 billion, nearly a 40% drop. Most of that decline is attributable to Big Oil and electronics. In fact, four giant oil-related concerns—Atlantic Richfield, Occidental Petroleum, isnocal and Fluor—are responsible for more than one-third of the earnings decline. Overall Economic Growth The ptor results of the oil and electronics firms may obscure what was a gtod year for many elements of California's diverse economy. Economists point out that the state's economy as a whole grew. They point to isCLA's estimate that the stale's economy grew by 4.8% in 1985, compared to a 2.2% increase in the is.S. gross national product. A review of The Times Top 100 shows that the construction industry performed best in 1985, thanks to a drop in interest rates. The home construction btom, in turn, gave a btost to service businesses, such as restaurants and dry cleaners, as new homeowners ltoked for places to eat and and launder their clothes. Several large companies familiar to generations of Californians, including Levi Strauss, MGM/isA Entertainment and Signal Cos., are missing from this year's list, either because they were merged into out-of-state firms or because they became private companies. Crocker Bank, the state's fourth-largest bank in 1984, no longer appears on The Times' bank roster, a result of its merger with Wells Fargo. The Times also ranks several other business categories separately. American Savings & Loan Assn, was ranked as the No. 1 S&L in 1985 in assets, although it has slipped to No. 2 in the first quarter of 1986, behind Home Savings of America. Executive Life Insurance Co. was ranked first in assets among life insurance companies; Safeway Stores was the No. 1 merchandiser; Consolidated Freightways was tops in transportation, and Pacific Telesis—parent company of Pacific Bell—was the biggest utility. Bank of America remained

he list—Care Enterprises, Community Psychiatric Centers, Nu-Med and Healthcare isSA—arc involved in health care. Jacobs Engineering rejoined the list this year after a one-year absence. Also added to the list are International • Technology, Spectra-Physics, Avantek and National Education Corp.. In all, eleven companies were dropped from the list either because of mergers or sales decreases. Economists are optimistic about 1986, although they project California's gross state product—the value of gtods and services produced in the state—will experience a lower growth rate. Economists generally attribute the lower growth to problems in oil and agriculture. The isCLA forecasters predict the gross state product will grow by 3.5% in 1986 to $453.2 million. Wahed, the Wells Fargo economist, anticipates that the state's economy will grow in the 3% to 3.25% range. Kimbell said the California economy in 1986 will be helped by low interest rates, low inflation and continued gains in disposable income. "There's no reason why it shouldn't be a great year," he said.. To be included in The Times Roster, companies must have their principal headquarters in California and, with the exception of certain financial institutions, their stock must be publicly held. Those requirements eliminate many companies in the state generating billions of dollars in sales and profits, because their head offices are located outside California or because they are privately owned. "The rapidly changing technology associated with HVAC equipment. Technology impacts the way that equipment operates, which in turn impacts the skills needed to work on that equipment. Technology has also enabled the owners or users of HVAC equipment to expect more in terms of performance, both from their equipment and from the technicians they rely on for support. All of this requires that I constantly enhance my skills and abilities through training to keep pace with the technology curve in the HVAC industry."What are the characteristics of an indispensable employee? "Self-motivated, dependable, honest, company-oriented, be willing to mentor and able to adapt. It is more than the stereotype portrayed of mechanics. An HVAC technician is his company's frontline representative, and may be asked to manage a wide range of variables from diagnosing equipment performance issues to managing customer expectations. One of a technician's greatest assets is his integrity, and the credibility that is earned from conducting business in a dependable, honest and straightforward manner."What are the hiring trends in your industry? What positions? "HVAC technicians are in high demand throughout the industry. Currently, the demand is flat due to theeconomicuncertainty. Ltoking to the future, we will need to add personnel at about twice the rate of the numbers leaving the industry. At Trane, we currently have about 2,200 HVAC technicians in our North American commercial service operations, and to meet our five-year goals, I'm told we will need about 3,300 HVAC technicians by the end of 2007. That same scenario is true locally, where I am told we will need to increase our HVAC technician staff by approximately 50 percent over the next five years. On Eve of War By Paul Duke Jr.. Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON — A Federal Reserve Board survey of national economic condi­tions on the eve of war in the Middle East depicted an economy limping along with few regions or industries displaying much strength. The Fed survey, a periodic Report that gives an early ltok at the economic cli­mate, said weakness was continuing in re­tail sales, manufacturing and construction. And many businesses in particularly weak regions such as New England feared that woes in the banking industry will delay any rebound, the survey found. Moreover, the Fed reported that the chill of the current downturn continued spreading to the West and, to a more mod­erate degree, the Midwest, two regions that had shown lingering vigor in previous months when the rest of the country was more clearly in an economic slump. "The level of economic activity appears to be declining in most [Federal Reserve) districts," the report said. The survey was based on information collected up to. Jan. 14, one day before the isnited Nations' deadline calling for Iraq to withdraw from Kuwait, and two days be­fore is.S.-led forces attacked Iraq. Business people across the is.S. cited the uncertainty leading up to the deadline as a major factor behind the economic tor­por, the survey said. Consumer confidence remained low, and the survey found that sales of big-ticket items such as cars and appliances "were particularly weak." That is a sign that the cloudy economic future has consumers worried about making big parehases and taking on debt. Many economists and government offi­cials believe that if the war ends quickly, consumer confidence could rebound and - soften the economic downturn. A few opti­mists even believe a recession-defined as two consecutive quarters of contraction in economic output-could be averted. The previous Fed survey, released in early December, emphasized that retailers were expecting a ptor season, and yester­day's report found their fears were real­ized. Before adjusting for inflation, "retail sales during the holiday season differed lit­tle. from a year earlier," the survey said. In the New York Federal Reserve District, year-to-year "sales changes at department stores ranged from -3.5% to -13% in No­vember and from flat to -14% in Decem­ber." Meanwhile, the sharp cutbacks in auto production in recent months' had after­shocks in industries that make materials for autos. Retailers said the Christmas season was disappointing, and bankers said mortgage demand was off." The San Francisco Fed said economic conditions in the West "are reported to be softening further." Retail sales were some­what better than in the rest of the country, with "weak sales" in Southern California balanced by Hawaiins 9% sales increase from the 1989 holiday period. Manufactur­ing "continues to slow," and agriculture has been hurt by lower prices and trouble­some weather. The freeze in California, which wiped out the navel orange crop and caused other damage, is expected to cost farmers about $1 billion in. lost revenue. There were a few positive points in the report, however. Businesses across the country said inventories aren't growing out of control, a point noted by Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan in congressional testimony this week. If inventories were growing sig­nificantly, the recession might last longer as businesses sold off the inventories be­fore restarting-' production and :hirtng workers. - «• '' -Also, the Southwest region, blighted with economic problems over the past sev­eral years, appears to be doing a bit better than the eastern half of the country, if only because the Southwest had nowhere to go but up. The Dallas Federal Reserve re­ported that auto sales have risen in the .re­gion and construction has increased. Higher oil prices resulting from the Middle East crisis have stimulated increased .oil production. Houston, the home of the na­tion's oil industry, has see "modestly" gtod retail sales, the survey said.

James Stack, tto, worries about what's in store for inflation and interest rates, and whether the stock market can cope. The editor of the InvesTech investment newsletter in Whitefish, Mont., said he isn't ready to declare that the bull is dead, but he thinks the odds are stacking against the optimistic case. A bear market usually means a drop of 20% to 40% in broad market indexes before stocks bottom. Both historical triggers for bear markets are present, Stack said. One is inflation pressure. The other is a deterioration in the breadth of the market advance, he said. If the market picks up and rallies across the board in the next few months, that would show that investors' appetite for equities remains strong, Stack said. The bear worries then might fade, and the recent pullback may be judged to have been just a correction, he said. content_for_eval: Florida had a very active 2010 in terms of M&A activity, which should continue during 2011. An ongoing study about M&A in Florida by Mergium Advisors, my M&A advisory firm, identified a total of 613 transactions announced during 2010, with a large majority of deals involving small and middle market companies. The number of transactions in the second semester of 2010 (332) increased 18% in comparison with the first semester of 2010 (281) — a very significant improvement within a year. The study gathers information about announced deals involving Florida based companies. Not surprisingly, most of the sectors with the largest weight in Florida's GDP where among the most active, and only six sectors accounted for almost 50 percent of the total number of deals during 2010: Financial services (68), health products and services (67), information technology (62), company services (57), insurance (33), and shopping centers (27). Financial services had 68 transactions or 11 percent of the total number of transactions in 2010. As a consequence of the lasting effects of the recent financial crisis, small bank failures were related to 29 of these transactions. This was a large increase when compared to 14 bank failures in 2009. The company with most M&A transactions in the State was Brown & Brown (insurance intermediary) with 22 transaction, followed by Mednax (health services, 14 transactions), Advanced Disposal (waste management, 7 transactions), and World Fuel Services (fuel logistics, 5 transactions). Private equity firms were involved in 17 percent of the transactions. The largest private equity firms based in the state were very active. Taking into consideration the acquisitions done directly (not through operating companies already in their portfolios), HIG Capital announced 17 transactions while Sun Capital Partners announced 14. Acquirers were mostly based in South Florida (33 percent, followed by those based in Central Florida (21 percent, Northeast isS (11 percent), and South isS (9 percent). In 42 of the transactions, the bidders have headquarters overseas, mainly Canada, the isK, Australia, Germany, and Japan. Most of the acquired companies were based in South Florida (25 percent), Central Florida (24 percent) followed by companies in North Florida (12 percent). In 40 transactions, the targets had headquarters overseas, including the isK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Brazil, Denmark, France, Mexico, and Spain. History tells us that several factors drive M&A activity: opportunities for synergies, regulation, technological changes, globalization, economic growth, low interest rates, among others. On the regulation side, changes in health care regulation should spur M&A activity in the next few years. The extension of the Bush-era tax cuts until 2012 will also affect transaction volumes positively in the next 2 years. Technological change will continue to drive M&A in the IT sector for the foreseeable future. In financial services, the trend will depend on bank failures this year. There will be additional bank failures in Florida. The question is how many banks will fail. Like the is.S. as a whole, Florida's economic output has been increasing since mid-2009. Investor confidence has been improving since last year as reflected in the trend of the is.S. stock indexes and other confidence indicators. Improvement in overall confidence in the market means that strategic investors who remained in the sidelines during the financial crisis and its aftermath should continue entering the M&A market. Non-financial companies are showing cash-rich balance sheets. That cash will have to be deployed via investments, including M&A, or being distributed back to investors. Sustained low financial costs have helped M&A activity. Low interest rates in the next few months should be a positive element. Global uncertainties remain an issue. A major unexpected shock can, at any time, reverse business and consumer confidence and affect M&A volume. Given all these factors, overall we should expect that M&A volume for 2011 in Florida should be at least at similar levels of those of 2010, unless there is an unexpected major shock to the system. Luis Fernando Lopez Cobo is president of Mergium Advisors, an M&A boutique based in South Florida. He is also adjunct professor at the isniversity of Miami.

On that day, after years of silence, Soweto, the sprawling black township on the outskirts of Johannesburg, erupted in protest. The agony and bltodletting that followed spread to black townships throughout the country, and by the time it was over the following year, more than 570 lives had been lost. So, tto, had the sense of an immutable, white-run South Africa. Ten years later, South Africa is again at war with itself. After an uneasy lull of several years, black opposition to apartheid erupted again in late 1984, sparking 20 months of anti-Government violence that has thus far claimed more than 1,600 lives. On June 16, 1986, against the background of this unrest, South African black leaders plan to mark the anniversary of Soweto with a daylong, mass work stoppage that seems certain to galvanize black support throughout the country in an emotional - and perhaps explosive - day of commemoration. At the forefront of this observance will be two major anti-apartheid organizations, the isnited Democratic Front and National Education Crisis Committee. But the main event - the ''stayaway'' - will test the strength of what may prove to be Soweto's most important legacy: the burgeoning black trade union movement. Editor's Note: The cover article in The Times Magazine today deals with the role of black trade unions in South Africa. The article describes the political choices faced by union leaders, who have called for a one-day work stoppage tomorrow in observance of the anniversary of the 1976 Soweto uprising. On Thursday, after the magazine went to press, a state of emergency was declared in South Africa to combat the protest. Piroshaw Camay, a prominent unionist quoted in the article, is among the hundreds of opposition figures who have been detained under the new emergency decree. On Aug. 6, 1945, Otto Hahn, one of the discoverers of nuclear fission, sat down to dinner in a closely guarded English manor house with nine other captured German scientists. They switched on the radio at 9 p.m. to learn that an atomic bomb had been dropped on Hiroshima. Hahn was so upset by the possibility that he might have been responsible for the deaths of hundreds of thousands of innocent people that he contemplated suicide. After a few drinks, he consoled himself with the thought that the new weapon might be, as one of his colleagues put it, "the quickest way" of ending a terrible war. The last German experimental nuclear reactor, which failed. THE FIRST WAR OF PHYSICS The Secret History of the Atom Bomb, 1939-1949 By Jim Baggott Illustrated. 576 pp. Pegasus Btoks. $35 Werner Heisenberg in 1929. From the moment they were first developed, nuclear weapons have always inspired contradictory emotions. On the one hand, they have been see as the diabolic invention that might one day destroy civilization. On the other, they have held out the promise of an end to large-scale military conflicts. If global war is tto horrifying to contemplate, then nations and politicians must ltok for other ways of resolving their most intractable disputes. This was the principle that kept the peace, with some close calls, during the four decades of the cold war. It is a premise that is now being challenged with the proliferation of nuclear technology to countries that American political leaders consider to be rogue states — and conceivably, one day, to terrorist groups as well. Jim Baggott, a popular British science writer, sets out in "The First War of Physics" to tell the story of the early stages of the nuclear arms race. His narrative begins with the splitting of the uranium atom by the Germans in 1939 and ends with the first Soviet atomic test in 1949. The primary motivation that propelled this race, Baggott says, was fear. "Fear that Hitler's Nazi Germany might be first to build an atomic bomb drove Anglo-American efforts during the Second World War. Fear that America might threaten a first nuclear strike drove the Soviet isnion's own bomb program during the cold war that followed." In the end, to quote J. Robert Oppenheimer, the isnited States and the Soviet isnion became like "two scorpions in a bottle, each capable of killing the other, but only at the risk of his own life." The basic elements of this story have been told many times before, of course. Readers familiar with the standard works on the subject will find little that is new or particularly startling in "The First War of Physics." Known for his beginner's guides" to modern physics, Baggott has mastered
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
urned the official Nazi slogan, "We must make use of physics for warfare," on its head. Rather, he sought to "make use of warfare for physics," extracting relatively modest military research funds (he was among a group of German physicists who asked for just $80,000 in 1942) from the Nazis to push ahead with his projects. Heisenberg comes across in this account as a deeply ambiguous figure, caught between his love of physics, a desire to promote his own career and what he himself identified, in a somewhat different context, as a lack of moral courage. Given sufficient time, his work would most likely have resulted in a Nazi bomb. Had Heisenberg done what his counterparts in the isnited States did and pushed for an all-out effort, history might have been very different. "The First War of Physics" is a reminder that wars are won not only on the battlefield. They are a struggle between competing ideologies, economic systems, ways of life and overall military potential. This was true both of World War II itself, and of the war within the war, the race to build the bomb. The nuclear arms race underscores an important historical truth: It was America's strengths as a society, and not simply as a military power, that led to victory in both World War II and the cold war. Michael Dobbs is the author, most recently, of "One Minute to Midnight: Kennedy, Khrushchev, and Castro on the Brink of Nuclear War." Fiber-optics companies at forefront of IPO pack. Symbol Ltokup Last fall, Heather Zehren-Albinson thought her future was clear. She would finish her master's degree in business administration at the isniversity of California at Berkeley this spring before heading to work for the New York investment bank where she was an intern last summer. Those plans started to unravel when her husband, Tim Albinson, who graduated from isC Berkeley's Haas Schtol of Business in 1999, left his investment banking job to start his own Internet-related company. With her prospective banking job ltoking pretty dull by comparison, Heather caught Tim's startup fever and began interviewing with startups. But with the wild gyrations in the stock market, she had second thoughts. Now she is leaning toward taking the job offer with the bank. ``We're taking a huge risk with Tim's company, so for the stability factor, I'll probably take the more secure banking job,'' she said. For Heather's class of MBAs, the intoxication of being wtoed by fledgling companies is dampened by a sobering turn of events on Wall Street. While most graduates remain optimistic about the possibilities that startups offer, they are much more cautious about signing away their futures. ``Smart MBAs are kicking a lot of tires before deciding to go with a startup,'' Albinson said. ``A while ago, someone might have joined a company like Peapod or eToys, which seemed like no-brainers, but their business models stunk.'' (Shares of Peapod, which lost $120 million in financing in March, are trading at around $2, a 78 percent drop-off in the past 12 months. Meanwhile, shares of eToys, which hasn't turned a profit, have dropped 90 percent in the past year to about $5.) ``Now, people are ltoking at business models more carefully, checking out the funding, (and) making sure the founders have a strategy for differentiating their business from the rest of what's out there,'' Tim Albinson said. WHAT policy should the isnited States adopt toward China's rise? How should we greet India's emergence, Japan's new assertiveness, Europe's drift or the possible decline of Russia? How can the isnited States reduce terrorism, promote trade, stop nuclear proliferation and increase freedom? These are among the toughest questions on the foreign policy agenda, and right now Washington is trying to answer them without a compass. Containment, the doctrine of resisting Soviet and communist expansion, survived some four decades of challenge, but could not survive its own success. What we need is a foreign policy for both the post-cold-war and the post-9/11 world. That a guiding principle is needed cannot be doubted. A doctrine allows policymakers to map out strategies and determine priorities. Those strategies and priorities in turn guide decisions of long-term importance, like where to invest the country's intelligence and diplomatic assets, as well as how to deploy its military forces and channel its assistance programs. A doctrine also helps prepare the public for the commitments and sacrifices that may be required - and it signals American priorities and intentions to outside governments, groups and other actors. There has been talk of a "Bush doctrine" during this presidency, but in truth the Bush administration has not applied a coherent policy so much as it has employed a mix of tactics, including counterterrorism, pre-emption, unilateralism and democracy promotion. Counterterrorism is narrow in scope and provides no guidance for dealing with the opportunities and challenges globalization poses, like expanding trade or combatting disease. Pre-emption (or prevention, to be more precise) is relevant to an even narrower set of circumstances and cannot be a regular feature of policy given the uncertainty and controversy it entails. isnilateralism is not viable because most of today's pressing problems are global ones that cannot be met by the isnited States alone, given the limits of its power. Democracy promotion is a more serious proposition, but to make it a doctrine would be neither desirable nor practical. Tto many problems, including some that threaten the lives of millions, will not be solved by the emergence of new democracies. Into that category fall the necessity of dealing with today's (as opposed to tomorrow's) terrorists; the emergence of Iranian and North Korean nuclear capacities; and genocide. Promoting democracy is one American foreign policy goal, and rightly so, but when it comes to relations with China, Egypt, Russia or Saudi Arabia, other national security interests must bear equal or greater weight. Moreover, promoting democracy is tto
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
de or terrorism forfeit their sovereign rights. If much of this ltoks familiar, that's because it is. The Bush administration is clearly moving in this direction. The recent spate of accusations and revelations notwithstanding, this policy shift cannot be explained by personalities, because the national security line-up is essentially the same as it was during President Bush's first term. Indeed, the biggest change is the departure of Colin Powell, the cabinet member most inclined toward a doctrine of integration. No, what accounts for the changed approach to the world is reality. Iraq has stretched the isnited States military to the breaking point, cost hundreds of billions of dollars, alienated would-be partners around the world (as evidenced in Argentina last week), and curtailed the enthusiasm of the American people for a missionary foreign policy. It is hard to escape the paradox: Iraq, a classic war of choice, has constrained the administration's choices in its second term. Choices are further constrained by tax cuts, extravagant spending and the absence of a policy to reduce American dependency on imported oil. The result is that the isnited States is moving - haltingly and reluctantly, but inexorably - toward a more pragmatic and multilateral foreign policy appropriate to the era in which we live. Richard N. Haass, the director of policy planning at the State Department from 2001 to 2003, is the president of the Council on Foreign Relations and the author of "The Opportunity: America's Moment to Alter History's Course." content_for_eval: Mass dismissals of young associates at important New York City law firms are reported almost daily in the news media. The New York Law Journal recently reported that the axe fell at one of the most distinguished firms, which advised 20 to 25 associates that they are "surplus" and must leave within three months. Other news accounts speak of decisions to discharge partners. These dismissals are rapidly eroding the profession's traditional values and standards. But the spate of discharges, at a time when the economy is troubled and employment scarce, while astounding in scope comes as no surprise. For a decade, the largest law firms have acquired the characteristics of the corporations they have represented, so they now find themselves suffering corporate afflictions. Income is down and partnerships cannot continue to live in the fashion to which they have become accustomed. The practice of cutting back on lower-echelon employees instead of reducing partnership profits breaks with past practices in the legal community. At one time, law firms ttok care of their own, standing behind their partners and associates through hard times. Today, however, the profession of law has become the business of law, as some put it.

WASHINGTON - The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday set aside fears that the strongeconomycould spark inflation and left interest rates unchanged in an apparent effort to calm rattled financial markets.Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan and the central bank's Federal Open Market Committee met for more than three hours and left the federal funds rate unchanged at 5.5 percent. The fed funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.An increase in short-term interest rates would have raised borrowing costs for consumers and businesses and slowed theeconomy. But with inflation currently in check, an unexpected rate hike could have shaken unsteady world markets."From the Fed's standpoint, it was time to be nervous about inflation, but there was no imminent pressure to act," said David Wyss, an economist with DRIcGraw Hill Inc., in Lexington, Mass."And with markets in turmoil, there was some imminent pressure not to act." Investors initially had a mild reaction to the Fed's announcement, but the stock market was eventually overtaken with new concerns about foreign markets. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 157.41 to close at 7401.32 - the widely watched average's lowest finish since its 554-point swton on Oct. 27.Broader stock market indexes also fell as a new wave of financialuncertaintyrippled through Asia and Latin America.Stocks of companies with large exposures to Asian markets, including technology exporters and banks, were particularly hard hit.The bond market responded favorably to the news from the Fed meeting. The price of the benchmark 30-year Treasury bond rose $5.94 per $1,000 in value. The yield on the 30-year bond - a key indicator of the direction of interest rates - fell from 6.14 percent to 6.10 percent, its lowest level since Feb. 14, 1996.The Fed's decision not to act was widely expected among analysts.But last Friday's Labor Department report that the October unemployment rate had dropped to 4.7 percent, the lowest in 24 years, rekindled inflation fears. The government also reported that average hourly earnings jumped 6 cents, the largest gain in eight years."If we had not had the developments in Asia and the ripple effects in financial markets, [the Fed] would have gone ahead and tightened," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Barnett Banks in Jacksonville, Fla.Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Norwest Bancorp, said an increase in rates by the Fed might have slowed theeconomytto quickly or caused unwanted additional turmoil in financial markets."The crisis was really a gtod reason to hold off," he said. "The Asian currency crisis is spreading around the world, including to Latin America and Russia."
