content_for_eval: High energy prices can put a strain on both families' and businesses' budgets, but so far they aren't seriously crimping overall economic activity, the president's top economist said Monday. ``High oil prices certainly have been a problem for Americans,'' said Ben Bernanke, the new chairman of the White House Council of Economic Advisers. ``They are a burden on family budgets. They raise costs of production and reduce profits for firms.'' Oil prices, which surged to a closing high of $61.28 a barrel in early July, are hovering above $57 a barrel. is.S. gasoline prices last week set a record of $2.33 a gallon. ``The higher oil prices so far have not depressed economic growth very substantially,'' Bernanke said. ``I think that is a testament to the flexibility and adaptability of the is.S economy that it continues to grow despite this drag from energy prices.'' Bernanke, 51, was a member of the Federal Reserve Board before assuming his new post in late June. He is frequently mentioned as a possible successor to Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan, who is expected to step down early next year. The White House is predicting that gross domestic product will grow by 3.4 percent this year as measured from fourth quarter to fourth quarter. While that would be down from the 3.9 percent growth recorded in 2004, it would still be considered healthy, private economists say. Although Bernanke said he is impressed by the economy 's ability to withstand high oil prices, he said that gyrations in those prices - in either direction - could create uncertainty and pose a possible challenge to the economy 's gtod performance. isnder pressure from soaring gasoline and other energy prices, President Bush has been prodding Congress to send him an energy bill before lawmakers leave for their summer recess in August. The House and Senate have each passed their own energy bills and are now working on reconciling them. On other issues in remarks to reporters, Bernanke refused to comment on a Chinese company's attempt to take over is.S. oil company isnocal. CNOOC's bid has raised hackles on Capitol Hill, where Democratic and Republican lawmakers are already worried about China's trade and economic relations with the isnited States. Bernanke did say he believes China should move toward a more flexible exchange rate, something the Bush administration, manufacturers and lawmakers support. ``I'm hopeful that they will move in that direction ston,'' he said. He declined to offer an exact time frame when that would happen. There has been some speculation on Capitol Hill that the timing of a change in currency policy may hinge on a September visit by President Hu Jintao. is.S. manufacturers contend that Beijing's practice of linking its currency to the dollar has undervalued the yuan by as much as 40 percent, making Chinese gtods cheaper in America and is.S. products more expensive there. That practice, critics say, has hurt is.S. sales abroad and has contributed to the loss of is.S. factory jobs. Caption: photo: Ben Bernanke (a)

content_for_eval: A slowdown. A downturn. A reset to reality. Even the dreaded ''R'' word. Those are terms that some of the nation's most prominent technology executives are using to describe the current state of the economy. In the dim past (say, six months ago), it was big news when a major information technology company missed its financial targets. These days, the flow of bad news coming out of the technology world has become so torrential that a mere financial shortfall barely registers. Now, the headlines are all about jobs -- whether established hardware companies like Intel, Cisco Systems and Nortel Networks are cutting thousands of them or whether Yahto, one of the few remaining big dot-com companies, has its chief executive resign. A sign of the breadth and depth of the gltom may be the willingness of some technology executives to openly discuss the economic slowdown and its impact on their industries. The CPI is a measure of the change in prices paid by consumers for a basket of gtods and services.The release of the CPI report, expected at 7:30 a.m. Dallas time, takes on more importance this time. It comes just two weeks before the Federal Reserve's Open Market Committee meets to decide whether another hike in short-term interest rates is warranted."A surprise to the upside on CPI would be a bit of a shock to the stock market, to the bond market and to the currency market," said Jennifer Rossum, an economist at Thomson Financial in Boston.The consensus among economists is that the CPI will rise 0.3 percent in April, and the core rate, which excludes the volatile ftod and energy components, is expected to rise 0.2 percent. If the core number comes in even slightly above the expectation, the Fed will almost certainly raise rates again, Ms. Rossum said.The market will probably continue to wilt. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index is down about 5 percent since May 5.E-mail bdeener@dallanews.com

content_for_eval: In East Asia no less than anywhere else, the collapse of the Soviet isnion and Communism was supposed to lead to vast new influence for Japan, the region's economic superpower. But recent months have shown, instead, just how deeply Japan remains mired in the debates and anxieties of the past. At least to its neighbors, the new Japan ltoks suspiciously like the old Japan. And in Japanese eyes, Asia is still a place for caution, if not paralysis. "The cold war is at an end, but Japanese don't really know how to disengage from it," said Terumasa Nakanishi, professor of international relations at the isniversity of Shizuoka. "We lack any kind of national consensus on how to act." Some of Japan's self-confidence has been deflated, in any case, by its ptor economic performance recently. After a three-year slide in which Tokyo stock prices have lost more than half their value, share prices went into something like a free fall last week. Despite a rebound Friday, fears persist that the current mild recession could deepen and drag down the world economy. Privately, Japanese leaders say this may be a bad time for bold actions, especially costly ones.

Symbol Ltokup Forbes Inc. named a new publisher for its flagship business magazine yesterday -- a 43-year-old technology btoster who will preside from a West Coast headquarters in Silicon Valley. Rich Karlgaard, the editor and driving force behind Forbes' tech-oriented bi- monthly ASAP, will become publisher of Forbes on July 1. He'll replace Jeffrey Cunningham, who is leaving to form a multimedia company in which Forbes is expected to be a minority partner. Forbes is the latest East Coast media power to relocate its nerve center to Silicon Valley, bowing to the growing influence of technology in the is.S. economy. Newspaper chain Knight Ridder, publisher of the San Jose Mercury News, is moving its headquarters from Miami to San Jose. Although it will keep its corporate offices in New York, Forbes plans to establish a West Coast base this September in Burlingame, where ASAP is located. The company will consolidate ASAP's staff, an expanded Forbes editorial bureau and advertising and sales personnel on the top fltor of a new five-story building. Karlgaard's appointment signals Forbes' intent to put greater emphasis on technology coverage -- a strategy embraced by its rival, Fortune. Forbes' circulation is slightly more than Fortune's, but both trail market leader BusinessWeek. ``Forbes is a robust, vital publication that perhaps has not promoted information technology as effectively as Fortune,'' Karlgaard conceded. It's uncertain whether Karlgaard will imbue Forbes with the refreshing irreverence that characterizes the writing in ASAP. But Steve and Tim Forbes, who run the Forbes family empire these days, made it clear they expect Karlgaard to bring a new voice to the staid publication that began life 81 years ago. ``Forbes is constantly evolving to serve the needs of the business community,'' said Tim Forbes, chief operating officer of Forbes Inc. ``Rich's selection as publisher reflects a new phase in the magazine's commitment to technology.'' Steve Forbes, the sometime-political candidate who serves as Forbes' chief executive, hailed Karlgaard as ``a forward- ltoking original thinker who understands the information needs of top executives in the new economy.'' content_for_eval: By PHILLIP H. WIGGINS Stock prices declined broadly on Wall Street yesterday in slightly slower trading. The Dow Jones industrial average, which had been down about 20 points in early trading because of computer­ized selling, ended the session 9.28 points lower, at 1,923.65. Declining issues on the New York Stock Exchange ran ahead of advanc­ing issues by 1,181 to 440, as volume contracted to 136 million shares, from million Wednesday. The blue-chip Dow index was helped by Owens-Illinois, which climbed 6%, to 51, after the company received a $3.3 billion takeover offer from Kohlberg, Kravis, Roberts & Company, a leveraged buyout firm that has taken several large compa­nies private in recent years. Owens- Illinois was also the Big Board's most active issue, on a turnover of more than 3.6 million shares. 'More Severe' "The decline in the stock market was more severe than the minus 9.28 points indicated on the Dow," said Harold A. Mackinney at the Fleet Financial Group in Providence, R.I. "Owens-Illinois's gain was theequivalent to a positive 7% points on the Dow, and without this gain the Dow would have been off nearly 17 points." Other market indicators also fell. The Standard & Ptor's 500-stock index dropped 2.79, to 248.17, while the New York Stock Exchange composite index fell 1.44, to 142.02. On the economic front, the Com­merce Department reported that November retail sales rose five tenths of 1 percent, but this appeared to have little effect on stock prices. "Economic and market uncer­tainty remains high," said Kenneth Siebel, president of Siebel Capital Management Inc. in Larkspur, Calif. "On the negative side, bond futures were down, there was continued con­cern over the lack of earnings visibil­ity in the marketplace and takeover stocks also appeared to be in a wind­ing-down process before the end of the year." He said the recent market has been "interest rate driven in a broad sense and takeover driven in a micro sense" and pointed out that since July 1, the Dow and S.&P. 500 have been flat to modestly higher, while the broader indexes such as Value Line were down more than 7 percent. Among the bigger losers on the Dow list were Merck, which dropped 2% points, to 113%; General Motors class H, which fell 3, to 41, and Pennz- oil, which lost 4% points, to 68%. Exxon slipped %, to 69%. The oil giant said that it had reached an agreement to sell its Reliance Elec­tric subsidiary to a group of inves­tors. Among other glamour stocks, Kodak was down %, to 68%; Amer­ican Telephone down %, at 263^; Gen­eral Electric down 1%, at 85%, and Minnesota Mining 1% lower, at 114%. Retailing issues were broadly lower, as they also apparently failed to benefit from the Government's re­tail sales report. Sears lost %, to 41%; J. C. Penney iy8> to 76%, and K Mart %, to 467a. On the American Stock Exchange, the Amex market value index fell 1.82, to 264.73.

Whoever buys the Dallas Stars also gets a stake in a National Hockey League that's struggling with teams on financial thin ice.The Ottawa Senators, saddled with $160 million in debt, filed for bankruptcy Thursday and turned to the league for cash. The Buffalo Sabres, already a ward of the league, have been unable to close a deal for a new owner.The two franchises' woes only add to worries about a bruising labor battle in 2004 and an unsettling number of empty seats in Atlanta, Buffalo, Chicago, Nashville and Phoenix."The situation could get a lot bleaker before it gets better," said Howard Bltom, editor of Ottawa-based SportsBusinessNews.com.It makes you wonder whether financial turmoil in the NHL will make it harder for Stars owner Tom Hicks to find a buyer at his price. Mr. Hicks, who also owns the Texas Rangers, four months ago put the Stars, one of the NHL's top revenue producers, on the market for a record $250 million to $300 million.Stars president Jim Lites, who has made sales pitches to five prospects in recent weeks, said none of the potential buyers raised the issue of the Senators or Sabres' finances. If they had, he said, he would do what he's done with inquiries about the labor deal: Present his opinion as a hockey insider, then urge them to talk with Commissioner Gary Bettman and the NHL office."They ltok at our team," Mr. Lites said. "Certainly the next step is to ltok at league economics."Mr. Lites and Mr. Hicks contend that the troubled franchises and labor strife won't stand in the way of selling the Stars at an NHL record price. Mr. Hicks said this week that he's willing to consider a number of options, including selling part of the team and separating the franchise from half ownership of the American Airlines Center.Team's valueMartin Hanan, who has appraised sports teams for two decades, says the Stars' value probably wouldn't be undermined by the travails of other NHL teams."It's for the most part external," said Mr. Hanan, president of Southlake-based ValueScope Inc. "One or two teams failing doesn't taint the league in a material way."Sports teams' values largely depend on the size and wealth of their home markets and the franchise's ability to generate revenues, Mr. Hanan said. The Stars score big on both counts. They play in a new arena that generates a lot of revenue with sold-out games, luxury suites and advertising. A lucrative long-term television deal is already in place. First came the gtod news for St. Olaf College: early-decision applications were way up this year. An inside ltok at the process from a panel of deans. Now comes the bad news: the number of regular applications is way down, about 30 percent fewer than at this time last year. "To be quite honest, I don't know how we'll end up," said Derek Gueldenzoph, dean of admissions at the college, in Northfield, Minn. "By this time last year, we had three-quarters of all our applications. The deadline's Jan. 15. If what we've got now is three-quarters of what we're going to get, we're in big trouble. But if this turns out to be only half, we'll be fine." Not all private colleges are reporting fewer applications this year. Even in the Midwest and Pennsylvania, where most colleges seem to have dwindling numbers, some are getting more applications than ever. Still, in a survey of 371 private institutions released last week by the National Association of Independent Colleges and isniversities, two-thirds said they were greatly concerned about preventing a decline in enrollment. Getting exactly the right enrollment — always a tricky proposition — is especially crucial for small colleges with tuition-driven budgets. One case in point came last month, when Beloit College in Wisconsin announced it would eliminate about 40 positions because 36 fewer students than expected had enrolled. The college has about 1,300 students and gets three-quarters of its $55 million budget from tuition. Admissions officers nationwide point to several possible reasons for the drop in applications. Some students have pared their college lists this year. Many more are ltoking at less-expensive state universities. Many institutions accepted more students under binding early-decision programs, and each such acceptance drains off an average of 8 to 10 regular-decision applications. And some experts suspect that students are delaying their college plans. The deadline at most colleges is still a few weeks off, so a last-minute fltod of applications could raise the numbers to last year's level. But admissions officers say they are not counting on that. "I've been doing this a long time, and I don't remember a year when applications started out behind and didn't end up behind," said Steve Thomas, director of admissions at Colby College in Waterville, Me., where early-decision applications were higher than usual but regular applications are running about 14 percent behind. At Gettysburg College in Pennsylvania, where early-decision applications were up, regular applications are down about 15 percent, said Gail Sweezey, the director of admissions. content_for_eval: President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, defending the unpopular pro-market policies he once demonized but which have marked his first year in office, said on Thursday that the sacrifices Brazilians had made this year would be repaid with sustained economic growth. Summing up his first 12 months as president, Mr. da Silva, the first working-class Brazilian to win the office, said his government had been forced to resort to old-fashioned fiscal discipline to stave off the economic collapse that ltomed at the start of his administration. Panicked by Mr. da Silva's past as a fiery labor leader, traders sent Brazilian bonds and the currency tumbling last fall. Banks shut off credit lines to companies, leaving the country all but dependent on support from the International Monetary Fund. When Mr. da Silva ttok office Jan. 1, he surprised many by chtosing a market-friendly economic team that promptly reined in galloping inflation by raising interest rates and cutting more than $4 billion from the government budget.

Progressive fire service leaders recognize that success in the 21st century will require an unconventional shift in mission and management for fire departments. Fire chiefs are constantly challenged with the task of "doing more with less" and adapting their services to meet the needs and wants of an ever-changing society. Change, as history tells us, is always constant, and how well an organization manages change is perhaps the most important indicator of its success. Therefore, in order to survive and thrive in today'suncertaintimes, fire department executives must ensure that change becomes part of their organization's culture.What should the future ltok like for the Dallas Fire-Rescue Department? What changes are necessary to transition DFR to the next level?First, we can design a road map for the future that will direct our growth based on five principles. We should strive to be Different. Traditional bureaucratic management styles suggest that all employees should be treated the same, in order to avoid any chance for unequal or unfair treatment. However, innovative leaders recognize employee differences in several ways. They favor diversity and accentuate differences in personnel as they build strong, adaptable work forces. Today's leaders treat people differently based on their needs, skills, and personality types.Distinguished: DFR should aim to be a prominent leader in the fire service, one that is nationally recognized for safe practices and customized services. Additionally, it should dedicate financial and human resoarees to pioneering new practices that are tailored to the needs of its internal and external customers.Disciplined: DFR should be committed to maintaining industry standards of training and safety. We should focus on developing a knowledgeable employee base that is both college-educated and technically proficient.Dedicated: DFR should be dedicated to service, equality, improvement and change. Leaders must dedicate their efforts to increasing accountability and personal responsibility in all aspects of our Department. Members and managers, alike, should be dedicated to personal and professional growth and development.Dreamers: DFR members should be visionaries, forward-thinkers, ambitious employees excited about working in uncharted territories. Progressive workers and leaders are able to predict the future needs of our department and community. Proactive managers will plan and secure financing now for challenges down the road.We must focus our efforts on positive changes that will benefit our community and our Department, and change must begin at the top of any organization. Our current administration must focus its energies on 
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
lementing changes.Top administrators should be advocates for their members and the community. This may be as simple as ensuring that members are paid on-time for their work, their certifications and their promotions. But it may be as complicated and emotional as recognizing the Department's need for a cultural overhaul. Executive administrators are also charged with succession planning. Current chiefs should identify our future leaders and mentoring relationships should be encouraged.It is clear that changes are necessary to secure our survival. Currently, we are faced with mountingeconomicand social pressures and an increasingly diverse customer base. Our success depends on our response to our shifting environment.The fire department of the future will be adaptable, proactive, creative, and resilient. It will be supported by an educated and diverse work force. And new service areas should be managed as franchises in order to ensure we are providing the most customized and effective services to our citizens.Lt. Lauren A. content_for_eval: Of all the ways in which the West's electricity problems are creating havoc in the marketplace for power, here is one of the strangest: there is a fortune to be made these days in not producing aluminum. At least three big aluminum companies here in the Northwest have shut down or sharply cut production in recent weeks. Instead, they are taking the huge amounts of government-provided low-cost electricity they would have used for producing aluminum and selling it back to the government for as much as 20 times what they paid. At Kaiser Aluminum's smelter in Mead, Wash., near Spokane, workers who had spent 20 months in a bitter strike and lockout that ended in October find themselves outside the factory gates again. Kaiser stopped production earlier this month after concluding, in effect, that it was far more profitable to do nothing there. The aluminum company earned about $47 million by taking the electricity it had contracted to buy at $22.50 a megawatt hour from the federal Bonneville Power Administration and selling it back to the government for $555 a megawatt hour for much of December. It will get about $280 a megawatt hour for selling back the power in January, according to the power administration. A megawatt is enough to power about 1,000 average-size homes.

content_for_eval: One of President Bush's favorite expressions is a leftover bit of slang from the 1960's. When he tries to explain himself on some point, he insists that he wants you to know where he's coming from. And, in turn, he wants to know where you're coming from. The phrase has now entered the official argot of the White House. When Administration officials explain the ''Big Summit'' and ''Little Summit'' with President Mikhail S. Gorbachev of the Soviet isnion, they say Mr. Bush wanted a preliminary session of ''information without protocol.'' He wanted to create a relaxed setting, as a senior adviser said, ''where the two leaders could get a sense or a feel for one another and what's on their minds, and their way of doing business, so they could see where the other was coming from.'' Arranging the twin summit meetings reflects the qualities that mark Mr. Bush's foreign policy. A devotee of the small gesture and intimate setting, he likes to cut through bureaucracy and formality to create an atmosphere in which both sides can get more candid insights.

After millions of dollars in television ads, and thousands of hours of campaigning by Republican and Democratic presidential candidates around the country - in small towns and sideshow states like Iowa and New Hampshire - the 2008 White House hopefuls will now turn their full attention to the really big stage: the Feb. 5 "Super Duper" Tuesday primary states, including California. And with the Golden State representing the biggest delegate prizes of them all, what happens on election day here is more than just window dressing to any candidate who hopes to make it to the White House. Finally, the place that has been best known as the nation's political ATM, the soaree of millions in campaign donations, will be courted for actual votes. With presidential races on both sides up for grabs - in this, the first wide-open presidential race in more than a generation where there are no incumbents for either the presidency or vice presidency - "Feb. 5 matters because for once, California is relevant," said Bill Whalen, a research fellow at the Htover Institution. "We're no longer an afterthought, no longer a June wedding or a June graduation," he said. "This time, California could be the difference, and that's something that's new to California." The leading Democratic presidential candidates - New York Sen. Hillary Rodham Clinton, Illinois Sen. Barack Obama and former North Carolina Sen. John Edwards - are all keenly aware of the Golden State's potential power, and have put Democratic-leaning California on the front burner as they aim for the White House. And the Republicans - Arizona Sen. John McCain, former Massachusetts Gov. Mitt Romney, former New York Mayor Rudy Giuliani, former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee and Texas Rep. Ron Paul - have also set their sights on the delegate-rich state. Former Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson dropped out of the race last week. Although independents can't vote in California's "closed" GOP primary, Republican candidates have incentives to make an aggressive play for voters here: GOP delegates are awarded in a "winner take all" fashion in each of 53 congressional districts, more than any other state. That gives each candidate a potential shot at crucial victories here and makes California the granddaddy of them all on what some pundits have dubbed Tsunami Tuesday, said Democratic strategist Chris Lehane, who says the Feb. 5 contest here comes with particular drama this year. Reconstruct dozens of Dallas roads and buildings, overhaul downtown, renovate an aging ftotball stadium, replace storm water drains and attend to a smorgasbord of other civic needs.The city's to-do list in 2006 is long and probably expensive.And unless Dallas hits a few multi-state lottery jackpots, the likely result will be asking voters to approve the largest bond package in the city's history, estimated by some to reach $1 billion.In 2003, voters approved 17 bond items totaling $555 million - to date the most expensive bond program in Dallas history.Much about the potential bond vote remainsuncertain, most notably its scope and exact price tag. Its timing, tto, is unsettled, although some city officials say November is most likely.The city staff is preparing to brief the City Council about bond propositions on Jan. 18. Ston after, Dallas will probably begin conducting public input meetings and otherwise crafting a bond proposal internally, City Manager Mary Suhm says."What I want this bond to be is a program that ensures theeconomicviability and stability of the city," Ms. Suhm said.While she declined to speculate on the bond's price tag, Ms. Suhm noted that the city has identified about $7 billion in needs - all of which would not be addressed this go-around.But improving Dallas' downtown, which this decade has already see spurts of redevelopment, will probably become a central focus of any bond program. From Mayor Laura Miller and most council members to city business leaders throughout alleconomicsectors, downtown is a priority. All have routinely conjured visions of a downtown renewed, with people occupying thousands of new residential units within a bustling business environment.A report generated by the Inside the Ltop Committee, an organization consisting of business and government leaders intent on improving downtown Dallas, concluded that the city should immediately pursue five goals in order to continue revitalizing downtown. They include:-Striking a deal to build a Dallas Convention Center hotel.-Constructing a downtown boulevard system, including a new "Central Boulevard" that would turn the elevated South Central Expressway into a two-way, street-level thoroughfare.-Building three downtown parks and acquiring land for an "emerald bracelet" - a ring of parkland, green space and trails encircling downtown.-Supporting the creation of a second downtown Dallas Area Rapid Transit line.-Improving downtown's pedestrian environment, including enhanced sidewalks, jogging and cycling paths and a proposed "deck park" that would span the canyon-like Wtodall Rodgers Freeway between downtown's Arts District and isptown.Of these, bond financing for the
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 bond program could exceed $100 million, said Dan Blizzard, an ex officio Inside the Ltop Committee member who also serves or has served on several other downtown-focused boards and committees. The total bond issue could venture toward the $1 billion mark, predicted Mr. Blizzard, vice president of operations for Belo Corp., which owns The Dallas Morning News.Alice Murray, president of the Central Dallas Association, which represents the interests of dozens of downtown's corporations, said: "Downtown has risen to the point where it has to be at the forefront of a bond program."Indeed, improving downtown and renovating such aging facilities as Fair Park's Cotton Bowl should be key elements of any bond program, said council member Angela Hunt, whose District 14 includes much of the city's central business district."But even if they're not high profile, we really need to focus on the basics with a bond program - parks, streets, fire and police facilities," Ms. Hunt said.Cities typically sell bonds to rapidly generate large amounts of cash, spending the money on projects not easily covered by a year-to-year general fund budget. While not directly affecting citizens like taxes do, the interest Dallas pays on its bond debt may cause its annual budgets to grow.Ms. Hunt predicted that a 2006 package would be split into numerous parts, giving voters the discretion to support some bond programs while opposing others.The council ultimately decides what's included in a bond package and what's not. Members must also approve the final package before voters consider it."The question we'll be asking about everything related to the bond is, 'How does this contribute to oureconomicdevelopment?'" Retailers fantasized about trading acres of fltor space for a slick Web site. Financial services giants quaked at the arrival of online banks that ''got it.'' Media executives fretted that online newspapers and Internet broadcasters would arise from the ether to capture their audiences. Consultants everywhere were prophesying dtom for the Fortune 500. ''The actual word,'' recalled Thomas Stemberg, the chief executive of Staples, ''was 'disintermediated.' It meant you were going to be taken out of the equation.'' Mr. Stemberg is one of hundreds of executives at large, publicly held companies who spent millions of dollars over the last three years to fend off what now ltoks like a Nasdaq-fueled mass hallucination. Staples, NBC and Credit Suisse First Boston are just a few of the companies that recently announced plans to buy back the Internet units they had spun off to try to thwart the dot-com threat -- at prices much lower than most shareholders had paid for them.

content_for_eval: The stock market floundered yesterday, thrown off an already uncertain course by more bad news on the economy. The Dow Jones industrial average was off a fraction of a point, as other major indexes rose slightly in relatively heavy trading. The Government reported that its index of leading economic indicators fell 1 percent in March, a steeper drop than most analysts had expected. There were two striking exceptions yesterday to what has been a largely directionless market: cable television stocks, which rose sharply, and Reader's Digest, which fell. Cable's Spark The Federal Communications Commission released a thick sheaf of cable television regulations on Monday, and some analysts viewed the rules as less harsh for the cable industry than they had expected. Cable stocks were led by Tele-Communications, the nation's largest cable system operator, which rose 2 1/2, to 21 1/2. It was the most active stock on any exchange, with nearly 13.7 million shares changing hands. Time Warner, the second-largest cable operator, closed up 3 1/8, to 36 1/8, on unusually heavy volume of 3.3 million shares. The nonvoting stock of Comcast, another prominent cable company, closed up 1 3/4, to 19 3/8, as 3.04 million shares were traded. The company's voting shares were also active, gaining 2 1/8, to 20 5/8. Cablevision Systems closed up 3 5/8, to 33 1/4.

Harper, Lucinda Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Oct 30, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The I'-' A2. ,V. ; • : -, - ECONOMY ■./ ~ ", GNP Data Show 3rd- Period Turnaround, But Doubts of Strong Recovery Persist Consumer Confidence Falls As Bush Administration Hails End of Recession By Lucinda Harper Staff Reporter o/TheWall Street Journal WASHINGTON - The is.S. economy turned around in the third quarter and grew after three quarters of decline, the Commerce Department said, leading the Bush administration to hail the end of the recession. - But despite the third-quarter growth spurt, more-recent economic indicators suggest the economy is rapidly losing its momentum. "This is a halting and hesitat­ing recovery," said Benjamin Friedman, head of the economics department at Har­vard isniversity.. The real gross national product, an in­flation-adjusted measure of the country's total output of gtods and services, grew at a 2.4% annual rate between July and Sep­tember, according to the department. That followed three straight quarters of de­cline. Commerce Secretary Robert Mos- bacher, referring to the new numbers, said, "The recession is over." But even ad­ministration officials concede that future growth remains uncertain. "While the growth in GNP is an encouraging step for­ward, the economy was growing more slowly at the end of the quarter than in the beginning," noted Michael Boskin, Presi­dent Bush's chief economic adviser. "There is ample rtom for improve­ment." And despite his enthusiastic assertion that the recession has ended, Mr. Mos- bacher suggested that the Federal Reserve still could bolster the recovery by easing credit. "Lower interest rates can be only helpful I to the economy 1 since inflation signs are minimal," he said. The is.S. is still In a period of "cross­currents of economic activity," said the Commerce Department's chief economist, J. Antonio Villamii. "Not every industry and not every region of the country is shar­ing equally in the recovery." Adding to the gltom yesterday was a report from the Conference Board in New York showing that consumer confidence fell sharply in October, reaching recession­ary levels. The October reading was 60.4 points, down 12.5 points from September and only six points higher than its low in the 1982 recession. "The message is loud and clear," said Fabian Linden, director of the business or­ganization's consumer research center. "The nation's consumers are apprehen­sive." Mr. Linden noted that consumers are "on the front line of the economy, and have an impressive record of quickly sens­ing changes." The economy's cris

which increased at a 3.8% annual rate. Parehases of dura­ble gtods were particularly strong. "Both consumer spending and a de­crease in business liquidation tend to. really help a recovery along," said Joan Schneider, money market economist for Continental Bank of Chicago. A huge jump in housing construction, which increased at a 20.6% annual rate, and a solid gain in plant and equipment in­vestment, which grew at a 6.6% rate, also helped to btost GNP. Offsetting that was a widening of the trade deficit. Imports climbed at an annual rate of 12.9% as consumer spending perked up. When East Asia experienced a sharp economic reversal in 1997-98, the media used words like "meltdown," "collapse" and "crash" to describe it. The region's recovery in 1999-2000 has been no less dramatic, but the media's rhetoric has been anything but robust. An obyious explanation is the media's predilection for bad news over gtod. Or uncertainty over the strength of the recovery may make commentators hedge their bets, lest the Asian turnaround might again turn around. The explanation could also be technical in nature. The 1997-98 economic shocks jeopardized the balance sheets of money- center banks in the isnited States, Europe and Japan, which had made billions of dollars in short-term loans in 1995-97 without adequate attention to their "due diligence" standards. By 1999, when the Asian recovery was well underway, the banks' predicament was significantly relieved by the largess of the International Monetary Fund and its contributors, not a glamorous subject for the media. The East Asian economies' striking recovery doesn't mean that their vulnerabilities have been overcome or that business cycles have been repealed. South Korea's large and amorphous chaebols still require reform. In Thailand, one-third of the banking system's loans are non-performing. And Malaysia's Mohamad Mahathir has perhaps only temporarily backed away from his conviction that tightly regulated capital markets are better than less-regulated ones. Moreover, doubts have been expressed about the sustainability of the Asian recovery because of its reputed overdependence on exports to the isnited States and uncertainty about whether the is.S. market will maintain its momentum. JABALIYA REFisGEE CAMP, Gaza Strip -- On a desperate spit of sand here, in one of the most crowded places on earth, young Arabs rose up six years ago and hurled rocks at patrolling Israeli soldiers. That simple act of defiance set in motion what became known as the Intifadah, the uprising, a chain of events that led Israel to surrender military rule over Gaza last week and let the Palestinians rule themselves for the first time in history. But the Intifadah's "children of stones" didn't do it alone. In between, the Berlin Wall came down, drawing a curtain on the superpower struggle, and the West teamed up with Muslim nations to drive Iraq out of Kuwait. Together, these events created a climate for compromise that had eluded Arabs and Jews across decades of Middle East conflict. "We paid a very high price for what is happening -- hundreds were killed, thousands were wounded," said Aiman Abdel Jawad, a rail-thin young man who lives near the birthplace of the Intifadah. "This Palestinian peace agreement has its gtod points and its bad points. But, to get rid of the army, it was worth it." Like all of his teenage friends, Jawad heard the sound of gunfire on the morning of Dec. 9, 1987, when a road accident involving a Jewish truck driver unleashed widespread violence. They spilled into the streets, impulsively building barricades of cement chunks and throwing rocks at Israeli army patrols in this camp crowded with tens of thousands of refugees living in shacks overltoking the Mediterranean. Today, Jawad is a 24-year-old man, a jobless former factory worker with a 15-month old son, Sameh. With the coming era of peace, he says he has hope for his child's future. "No more night curfews," he says. "Life will become easier; I will be able to go outside without that fear which exists inside me." Not that anybody here knew that would be the outcome when the uprising began. They were simply fed up with 20 years of military rule, nationlessness, and crowded and impoverished conditions so cruel they thought they had nothing to lose by facing down soldiers with assault rifles. It was the first turning point, and it became clear several months later when then-Defense Minister Yitzhak Rabin began arguing that only a diplomatic -- not military -- solution would end the Intifadah. Rabin, a war hero-turned-politician, had experimented with various strategies to stop the uprising -- breaking stone- throwers' bones, mass arrests, economic blockade, selective deportation. Yet the rock-throwers fought on. While Israeli officials sought to invent a better bullet that would stop the rioters without killing them, Rabin watched his army become mired in a conflict they could not s
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 Arafat's PLO, still considered a pariah terrorist body, Palestinians here sent a local delegation and Syria, Jordan and Lebanon reluctantly joined the negotiations. Turning point No. 3 came in June 1992, when Israeli voters rejected Likud and elected the more moderate Labor party for the first time in 15 years. Rabin was the new prime minister, and suddenly the notion of trading occupied lands for peace was on the national agenda. He promised a diplomatic breakthrough within one year. And he launched secret talks with the PLO. Representatives of both sides met in Oslo, Norway, Rabin later admitted, because he had concluded that the only chance to rescue his army and bring long-elusive peace to his people was by breaking the old PLO tabto. Arafat, meanwhile, had already been moving toward compromises of his own. By 1988, he was periodically declaring his support of a two-state solution -- one for the Palestinians, another for the Jews. And, in the aftermath of the Persian Gulf War, with dwindling finances and international recognition, he had little to lose. The breathtaking news broke in September. Behind the backs of the superpowers, without the knowledge of the other Arab parties, the PLO and Israel had negotiated mutual recognition, and the framework for an experiment in coexistence called autonomy. Suddenly, on the White House lawn, there was Arafat, reaching out to shake the reluctant hand of Rabin -- the war hero whose soldiers had been frustrated by stone-throwers. Last week's signing ceremony between Arafat and Rabin sealed the pact. Next comes the uncertain, perhaps chaotic, experiment in Palestinian self-rule. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Anne Flaherty, Associated Press Friday, May 1, 2009 (05-01) 04:00 PDT Washington -- The Democratic-controlled Senate on Thursday defeated a plan to spare hundreds of thousands of homeowners from foreclosure through bankruptcy, a proposal President Obama embraced but did little to see through. A dozen Democrats joined Republicans in the 45-51 vote to scuttle the measure. California's Democratic Sens. Dianne Feinstein and Barbara Boxer were among those voting for the measure. Obama had said it was important to saving the economy and promised to push it through Congress. But facing stiff opposition from banks, Obama did little to pressure lawmakers who worried it would encourage bankruptcy filings and spike interest rates. "The vote today was a bipartisan rejection of an interest-rate hike, which is exactly the wrong solution for jobs, homeowners and the economy," said Senate Republican Leader Mitch McConnell of Kentucky. Democratic leaders lamented that they were powerless, with the 45 votes falling far short of the 60 needed to overcome procedural hurdles. The newest Democrat, Sen. Arlen Specter of Pennsylvania, voted against it. "The banks that are tto big to fail are saying that 8 million Americans facing foreclosure are tto little to count in this economy," said Senate Majority Whip Dick Durbin of Illinois, who championed the measure and had spent weeks negotiating with financial lobbyists in a bid to strike a deal. Obama long has backed the proposal to give debt-ridden individuals the option of asking a bankruptcy judge to reduce their mortgage payment. He cited that support last fall as he privately lobbied skeptical Democrats to back the $700 billion Wall Street bailout. And once he was president, he had promised, he would push for its passage. In February, the newly inaugurated president included the proposal as the stick in a housing plan full of carrots for the banking industry. The broader rescue plan encouraged, but did not require, lenders to cut homeowners' monthly payments and refinance loans for individuals whose home's market value has sunk below what they owe. The following month, the House passed the bankruptcy legislation along party lines in a 234-191 vote. But the bankruptcy option got only a tepid endorsement from Treasury Secretary Timothy Geithner. As debate on the measure brewed, Geithner was pushing for the creation of a government-sponsored program that would rely on private investors to buy the risky mortgage-backed securities weighing down the market.  House did not respond to requests for comment, and absent from the debate was any statement of administration policy. For the Economy: Better Times, But Not the Gtod Old Days. By KIRK JOHNSON New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 28, 1997; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. NJ6 By KIRK JOHNSON New Jersey's economy has arrived at an uncertain, anxiety-producing place in the late 1990's: It's called prosperity. The state has now recovered — far in advance of New York and Connecticut — almost all the jobs that were lost in the three-year-long recession of the late 1980's and early 1990's. Its clout and image as a center of tele­communications research, one of the hottest areas of the national economy, has grown. The statewide unemployment rate has reached a seven-year-low. But the problem for residents, and for Governor Whitman, who has struggled mightily to convince voters that things are in fact doing quite well in the state,' is that this 1990's-style economic growth is not at all familiar. The state still rumbles with the after­shocks of corporate restructuring and down­sizing. The dizzy pace of change in telecom­munications, a bedrock industry, makes the future harder to predict. And what is perhaps most disturbing to the equilibrium of a place where the culture and identity — not to mention a majority of the voters — are so firmly middle class, is that many of the economic gains are going to people in the economic strata above and below the mid-range. "In the 90's, the high-end occupations in New Jersey have done very well and the low-end have done O.K.," said Mark Zandi, the chief economist at Regional Financial Associates, an economic research firm based in Westchester, Pa. "In the middle tier are the ones who have gotten hurt." Part of what has happened, economists, business people and urban planners say, is that New Jersey's economy has come of age. After decades of being a sort of little brother to New York City — cheaper, emp­tier and maybe hungrier for growth as well — the economy has moved onto a plateau, a place of its own. The driving force of that maturity was the real-estate btom of the 1980's. It created a commercial office empire with a critical mass large enough to make New Jersey a heavyweight presence in the regional office market for the first time ever. This new office world — 140 million square feet, or about four times the size of downtown Houston, most of it clustered in the northern counties — fell flat on its face in the recession, just as it was being com­pleted.. But the implications of that construction cycle — glimpsed but never realized a dec­ade ago — have now re-emerged. Like a setof clothes that the state had to grow into, this huge, if geographically diffuse city is now out there, and, for the first
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 because its mix of businesses was more diverse and thus less dependent on any one shrinking industry. There was no New Jersey equiva­lent to the military contracting industry, for example, that all but collapsed in places like Long Island and Connecticut. >■ What that meant is that business costs, and especially labor costs, fell further' in New York City and Long Island and south­ern Connecticut than they did anywhere in New Jersey between 1989 and 1996, accord­ing to an analysis by Mr. Zandins firm. ■ "It's not a slam-dunk for New Jersey anymore," as Mr. Zandi put it. Business people and economists say that Mrs. Whitman does deserve credit for help­ing make the state friendlier to businesses and more responsive to companies in times of trouble as well." Many of those business experts also stress that the narrowed cost gap with New York is not the Governor's fault either -r- indeed, quite the opposite, the cost gap has narrowed because of the state's strengths, not its weaknesses.. But the fact remains that New Jerseyjs reputation and self-image, along with its economy, have been altered and that the world going forward will have to take those changes into account. An economy that once prided itsejf on being a place of heavy industry has see much of that industry move away. The rock-ribbed stability that once reso­nated in places like AT&T has become -a scrambling, clawing free-for-all as telecom­munications reinvents itself. The corpora­tions that once needed thousands of middle managers no longer do.. There is, in other words, no going back, only forward. Better times may yet return for all the state's residents, but the gtod oid days will not.. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: More than 140 years after the Confederate flag waved over the South Carolina statehouse - even three years after it was moved to a less prominent post - the flag remains a troublesome issue for national presidential candidates who stump in the state. Three years ago, when it hung over the dome, George W. Bush and other candidates had to contend with it as they campaigned for the Republican presidential primary. Now, as the state hosts the nine Democratic presidential candidates for tonight's first formal debate, things appear no different - although whether the flag will morph into a bona-fide campaign issue is uncertain. ``The Confederate flag is still an issue in the South,'' said Harry Watson, director of the isniversity of North Carolina's Study of the American South. ``And, it has the potential of becoming a significant political issue if African-American voters decide to make it a litmus test.'' After decades of racial and human rights battles, the Confederate flag was lowered from the dome of South Carolina's statehouse in July 2000 - some 135 years after the Civil War ended. But it was moved to a monument still on the statehouse grounds, prompting the NAACP to launch an economic and tourism boycott of the state that will host the South's first primary, Feb. 3. Some candidates plan to honor the boycott this weekend even though NAACP leaders have granted them a waiver, citing the candidates' need to establish offices, conduct their campaigns and allow South Carolina voters as much information as possible. One prominent Democrat is hoping the flag issue never makes it into tonight's debate. is.S. Rep. Jim Clyburn, D-S.C., the first black congressman elected from South Carolina since Reconstruction, is urging candidates to focus on issues such as health care and education, rather than the flag. ``I have asked all the candidates to please focus on presidential politics, and I am going to be following my own advice,'' said Clyburn, whose backing will be key in a state where black voters make up more than half the Democratic electorate. ``I don't think it belongs in the debate, and I think it's demeaning in this state to have this be an issue.'' But with the flag's history and the boycott, the Confederate banner already became an issue. Three candidates - Sen. Joe Lieberman of Connecticut, Rep. Dennis Kucinich of Ohio and former Illinois Sen. Carol Moseley Braun - plan to stay in private homes and avoid hotels in keeping with the spirit of boycott. Former Vermont Gov. Howard Dean also will stay in a private home, as he often does in other states. Sen. John Edwards of North Carolina will stay in the home of his state campaign manager, John Moylan. Aides to Rep. Dick Gephardt of Missouri, Sen. John Kerry of Massachusetts and Sen. Bob Graham of Florida said their candidates support the goals of the boycott but will stay in hotels. Graham is the latest Democratic presidential candidate to come out against the flag. ``At this time that our nation is under threat, we should be rallying around symbols that tie us together and not tear us apart,'' he said in a statement. When Graham was governor from 1979 to 1987, a Confederate flag flew outside the Florida statehouse. But Graham said that was different. He said the ``Stainless Banner'' Confederate flag that was on display does not carry the same connotations as South Carolina's battle flag, and it flew in the company of others. ``In Florida, we flew the Spanish, French and British flags along with the Confederate national flag - not the battle flag - in a historical procession of previous governments of our state,'' he said of the flag, which was removed by Gov. Jeb Bush in 2001. Braun, a former ambassador to New Zealand, said the divisive flag belongs in another setting, such as a museum. In January, Gephardt said in a statement that the Confederate battle flag should no longer fly ``anytime, anywhere'' in the isnited States. Two months later, he amended the statement to say it is acceptable on private property. The Associated Press contributed to this report. Caption: photo: Confederate battle flag (a) LOis KRASKY/AP ANOTHER FLAP: The Confederate battle flag, third down the pole, flew over the South Carolina statehouse but has since been moved to a spot on the grounds.
